Here's the full picture of your codebase:

---

## Project: "Battle Arena" — A Social Challenge Platform

A **full-stack application** with a **Flutter frontend** and a **Go backend**, designed as a competitive social platform where users challenge each other (video/skill-based).

---

### Frontend (Flutter — `lib/`)
**30 Dart files** using **Provider** (ChangeNotifier) for state management and **Material 3** theming.

| Layer | Files | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Entry** | `main.dart`, `main_shell.dart` | MultiProvider setup, 5-tab bottom nav (Home / Search / Create / Arena / Profile) |
| **Models** | `user_model`, `post_model`, `challenge_model`, `comment_model`, `notification_model` | JSON-serializable data classes |
| **Providers** | `auth_provider`, `data_provider`, `feed_provider`, `theme_provider` | Auth state, user/followers/notifications, paginated feed, dark/light mode |
| **Services** | `api_service` (REST), `websocket_service` (WS) | Centralized HTTP client + persistent WebSocket with auto-reconnect |
| **Pages** | `home_feed`, `search`, `profile`, `challenges`, `challenge_detail`, `create_challenge`, `notifications`, `followers`, `following`, `upload` | Full UI for browsing, creating, and interacting |
| **Widgets** | `post_card`, `comment_sheet`, `user_tile`, `league_badge` | Reusable components with optimistic like/follow UX |

**Key patterns:** Optimistic UI updates (likes, follows revert on API failure), real-time notifications via WebSocket, mixed grid feed (3 challenges + 1 post).

---

### Backend (Go — `devb/`)
**11 Go files** using **Gorilla Mux** + **PostgreSQL** + **Redis/Valkey** + **Gorilla WebSocket**.

| Layer | Files | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Server** | `main.go` | Route definitions, CORS, startup init |
| **Models** | `models.go` | User, Post, Challenge, Comment, Notification structs + request payloads |
| **Database** | `database.go` (1015 lines) | PostgreSQL CRUD, batch enrichment, seeding (10 users, 26 posts, 8 challenges) |
| **Handlers** | `event_handler.go`, `feed_handler.go`, `challenge_handler.go` | REST endpoints for social actions, feeds, and challenges |
| **Services** | `event_service.go`, `notification_service.go` | Follow/unfollow logic, hybrid online/offline notification delivery |
| **Real-time** | `websocket.go` | In-memory client map, ping/pong heartbeat (54s/60s), cleanup on disconnect |
| **Cache** | `redis.go` | Offline notification queue (30-day TTL), fetch-and-clear on reconnect |
| **Search** | `search.go` | Levenshtein fuzzy matching with multi-signal scoring (exact > prefix > fuzzy > token > name) |

**Key design:** Notifications route to WebSocket if online, Redis if offline (delivered on reconnect). Paginated feeds (max 50). Connection pooling (25 open / 5 idle). Seeded demo data.

---

### Data Flow
```
Flutter UI → Providers → ApiService (HTTP) / WebSocketService (WS)
                              ↓                       ↓
                    Go Handlers → Database (Postgres)
                              ↓
                    NotificationService → WebSocket (online) / Redis (offline)
```

The backend is deployed at `gobackend-9nd8.onrender.com`. The app features **leagues** (Bronze → Diamond), **challenges** with arena/friends visibility, and a **search** with typo tolerance.

── USER @ 2026-03-29T10:20:01.658Z ──
How to make the feed addictive, How to make them feel happy, enjoy, fun, pleased, relax, if he is tired then tired free, motivated, emotional after what kind of content anybody seem that makes him happy, enjoy, fun, pleased, relax,  if he is tired then tired free, motivated, emotional, also that will release dopamine? means all the psychologies that can be used to spend more time on the app by the user.



This is a very serious and powerful question, and you’re thinking in the right direction — not like a coder, but like a behavior designer. I’ll answer this in a psychology-first way and then map it to feed & product design, especially relevant to your gamified social app.



⚠️ Small note: There’s a thin line between addictive and harmful. The best apps make users feel better after using them, not empty. I’ll focus on healthy dopamine, not dark patterns.



1. First Principle: Why Humans Open Feeds Repeatedly



People don’t open apps for content.

They open apps for emotional state change.



They want to move from:



😴 tired → refreshed

😞 bored → interested

😔 lonely → connected

😐 neutral → excited

😕 unmotivated → inspired



Your feed should promise a mood transition, not just videos.



2. Dopamine Basics (Very Important)



Dopamine is NOT pleasure.

Dopamine = anticipation + reward prediction.



That’s why:



Scrolling feels better than watching one long video

“Next” is stronger than “Now”

Mystery beats certainty



Core rule:



Never give full emotional closure too quickly.



3. Content Buckets (Psychological Triggers)



Your feed should rotate emotional buckets, not random posts.



🔁 The 6 Core Buckets

1️⃣ Micro-Joy (Instant Dopamine)



Goal: Quick smiles



Content:



Cute animals

Satisfying loops

Small wins

Funny mistakes

Relatable memes



🧠 Why it works:



Zero thinking

Fast dopamine

Low energy consumption



📌 Use when user is:



Tired

Scrolling fast

Late night

2️⃣ Relatability (Emotional Bond)



Goal: “This is literally me”



Content:



“POV” videos

Daily struggle content

Relationship humor

Work/student life pain



🧠 Why it works:



Activates mirror neurons

Reduces loneliness

Builds emotional attachment to the app



📌 Makes user feel:



“This app understands me”



3️⃣ Motivation / Hope



Goal: Energy boost



Content:



Transformation stories

Comeback arcs

Gym, business, glow-up

“I was broke → now…”



🧠 Why it works:



Dopamine + meaning

Gives future reward vision

Makes users stay longer



⚠️ Important:

Motivation should be short & raw, not preachy.



4️⃣ Curiosity & Mystery



Goal: “Wait… what happens next?”



Content:



Incomplete stories

“Nobody talks about this…”

Unexpected twists

Secrets / reveals



🧠 Why it works:



Triggers Zeigarnik Effect

Brain hates unfinished loops



📌 Design tip:

Split content into:



Part 1 (hook)

Part 2 auto-next

5️⃣ Emotional Release (Deep Dopamine)



Goal: Emotional cleansing



Content:



Reunions

Kindness moments

Sad → happy transitions

Real human vulnerability



🧠 Why it works:



Oxytocin + dopamine

Strong memory formation

Users remember your app emotionally



📌 Use sparingly:

Too much = emotional fatigue



6️⃣ Status & Ego Boost



Goal: “I matter”



Content:



Likes

Challenges

Leaderboards

Wins / streaks



🧠 Why it works:



Status = survival instinct

Dopamine spikes when ego is validated



🔥 This fits PERFECTLY with your challenge + league system.



4. Feed Flow Psychology (VERY IMPORTANT)

❌ Bad Feed



Random emotions → confusion → fatigue



✅ Addictive Feed



Emotional Wave Pattern



Example flow:



Funny → Relatable → Curious → Motivating → Funny → Emotional → Ego Boost



This prevents:



Burnout

Emotional saturation

Boredom

5. Interaction = Dopamine Multiplier

Passive scrolling = weak dopamine

Active participation = strong dopamine



Add:



Tap to reveal

Vote left/right

Choose winner

Predict outcome

Swipe decision (yes/no)



🧠 Brain loves micro-control



6. Tired User vs Energetic User

Detect energy level by:

Scroll speed

Time of day

Interaction rate

😴 Tired User Feed

More micro-joy

Less text

Softer music

Slower transitions

🔥 Energetic User Feed

Challenges

Competition

Fast cuts

Motivation

7. “One More Scroll” Mechanism



The strongest addiction trigger:



Near-Miss Dopamine



Examples:



“You were 2 likes away from winning”

“Next video decides the winner”

“One more challenge unlock”



Casino psychology — but ethical.



8. Make Users Leave Happy (This Is Gold)



Most apps fail here.



Before exit:



Show small win

Show progress

Show appreciation



Example:



“You gained 14 points today. Nice work 💙”



This creates:



Positive memory

Return habit

Emotional trust

9. Emotional Safety Rule (VERY IMPORTANT)



Never:



Over-stimulate constantly

Shame users

Punish inactivity harshly



Why?

Burnout kills retention faster than boredom.

Exact feed ranking formula

Scroll gesture psychology (you asked earlier — I remember)

Challenge loop design

Anti-burnout algorithm

Daily emotional journey map

Ethical addiction vs dark patterns

I’ll explain this like a behavior psychologist + product architect, and I’ll connect it to your challenge-based social app so this is actionable, not theory.

1️⃣ Scroll Gesture Psychology



(How movement itself controls dopamine & emotion)



Gestures are subconscious commands. The brain reacts before logic.



Vertical Scroll (Up/Down) → Time Dissolution



Used by: TikTok, Reels



🧠 Psychology:



Feels infinite

No mental “end”

Brain loses time awareness



✅ Best for:



Passive relaxation

Tired users

Night usage



⚠️ Risk:



Fatigue

“What did I even watch?” feeling

Horizontal Swipe (Left/Right) → Decision & Control



Used by: Tinder, challenges



🧠 Psychology:



Binary choice = control

Ego activation

Higher dopamine per action



✅ Best for:



Challenges

Voting

Competitive content



🔥 Tip for your app:



Left = skip

Right = engage

Never mix with system back gestures

Tap / Hold → Anticipation Dopamine



🧠 Psychology:



“I choose when reward comes”

Strong anticipation loop



Examples:



Tap to reveal result

Hold to unlock next part

Long press for bonus



This feels less addictive, more satisfying.



Multi-Gesture Fatigue Rule (CRITICAL)



Never require 2 different gestures for same intent.



❌ Bad:



Swipe up sometimes = next

Swipe left sometimes = next



🧠 Brain hates uncertainty in control.



✅ Rule:



One gesture = one outcome forever

Gesture Hierarchy (Recommended for You)

Gesture	Meaning

Swipe up	Next content

Swipe down	Exit / rest

Swipe right	Engage / accept

Tap	Reveal

Hold	Earn / unlock



This prevents gesture anger, which kills retention silently.



2️⃣ Anti-Burnout Algorithm



(Why users quit & how to stop it)



Burnout ≠ boredom

Burnout = overstimulation without emotional reward



Burnout Signals (Track These)

Scroll speed increasing

Likes decreasing

Shorter sessions

Late-night passive usage

Skipping emotional content

Anti-Burnout Logic (Core Concept)



Instead of:



Maximize watch time



Do:



Maximize emotional recovery



Algorithm Layer 1: Energy Detection

If scroll_speed > fast AND interaction < low

→ User tired

If interactions high AND watch_time high

→ User energetic

Algorithm Layer 2: Content Cooling

😴 Tired User Feed

Micro-joy

Calm music

No challenges

Short loops

🔥 Energetic User Feed

Competition

Challenges

Motivation

Wins

Algorithm Layer 3: Emotional Saturation Cap



Limit per session:



Max 2 emotional-heavy videos

Max 3 motivation pieces

Insert neutral content after intensity



🧠 Brain needs emotional breathing space



Algorithm Layer 4: Graceful Exit



After X minutes:



“You’re doing great today. Want to continue or take a break?”



⚠️ This increases retention, not decreases it.



Users trust apps that don’t trap them.



3️⃣ Daily Emotional Journey Map



(This is where most apps fail)



People feel different emotions at different times.



Morning (Hope & Identity)



🕘 6–10 AM



Emotions:



Fresh

Goal-oriented



Feed:



Motivation

Wins

“Today you can…”

Afternoon (Light Distraction)



🕛 12–4 PM



Emotions:



Mild boredom

Work fatigue



Feed:



Funny

Relatable

Quick dopamine

Evening (Social & Ego)



🕕 6–9 PM



Emotions:



Comparison

Social validation



Feed:



Challenges

Leaderboards

Social wins



🔥 Best time for your league system



Night (Comfort & Emotional Safety)



🕙 9–1 AM



Emotions:



Vulnerable

Tired

Reflective



Feed:



Calm

Emotional closure

Kindness content



❌ Avoid:



Harsh competition

Aggressive motivation

Golden Rule



Match feed emotion to real-world emotion, not random content.



4️⃣ Ethical Addiction vs Dark Patterns



(This determines long-term success)



Dark Patterns (Short Life, High Damage)



❌ Infinite intensity

❌ Guilt-based retention

❌ Fear of missing out manipulation

❌ Punishing inactivity

❌ No exit signals



Result:

📉 Burnout

📉 Hate usage

📉 App deletion



Ethical Addiction (Long Life, Loyalty)



✅ Predictable gestures

✅ Emotional variety

✅ Visible progress

✅ Rest encouragement

✅ User feels better after session



Result:

📈 Habit formation

📈 Trust

📈 Long retention



Simple Test (VERY IMPORTANT)



Ask:



“Does the user feel better or worse after 20 minutes?”



If worse → dark pattern

If better → ethical addiction

People don’t remember:



Videos

Features

Algorithms



They remember:



How your app made them feel when they closed it



That emotional memory decides:



Re-open

Recommend

Perfect. Now we are entering core algorithm + behavioral inference, not UI tricks.

I’ll answer each question step-by-step, moving from content understanding → user understanding → dynamic emotional orchestration. This is exactly how TikTok/Instagram/YouTube Shorts think internally.



I’ll keep this practical + implementable, not academic.



1️⃣ How can you know which short is relatable to a user?



Relatability is not a tag, it’s a reaction pattern.



Relatable ≠ liked



Relatable = “This matches my internal state or life context”



Behavioral Signals of Relatability (STRONG)



If a user:



Watches longer than average

Rewatches a section

Pauses briefly

Saves but doesn’t like

Sends to close friends

Comments “me”, “same”, “literally me”



🧠 That’s relatability, not entertainment.



Algorithmic Relatability Score (Simple)



For each user × content:



RelatabilityScore =

  (WatchTime / VideoLength)

+ PauseEvents * 0.3

+ Rewatch * 0.5

+ ShareToFriend * 1.0

+ CommentRelatableKeywords * 0.8



Likes are weak signals

Watch behavior is truth



Personal Relatability Memory



Store:



UserRelatableProfile:

- Topics

- Emotions

- Content pace

- Creator types



Example:



User relates more to “struggle + humor” than “success flex”



This becomes personal emotional fingerprint.



2️⃣ How to know under which type a short comes (joy, emotional, etc.)



You do multi-layer classification, not one tag.



Layer 1: Creator Intent (Soft Signal)



From:



Caption keywords

Music choice

Hashtags



This is weak, but useful initially.



Layer 2: Content Features (Medium Signal)



Automatically extract:



Music tempo (fast = joy, slow = emotional)

Color warmth

Face expressions (smile / crying)

Cut speed

Text tone



This gives initial emotion guess.



Layer 3: Audience Reaction (STRONGEST SIGNAL)



Emotion is defined by how people react, not what creator intended.



Reaction → Emotion Mapping

User Behavior	Emotion Type

Fast likes + smiles	Joy

Long watch + silence	Emotional

Commenting stories	Relatable

Shares + DMs	Connection

Replays	Curiosity

Scroll away fast	Mismatch



After ~100–300 views, emotion label stabilizes.



Final Truth:



Content emotion is emergent, not declared.



3️⃣ How can you know the user better (Mood Detection)



You never ask the mood.

You infer it silently.



Mood = Energy + Emotion + Intent

3.1 Energy Detection (Most Important)

Signals:

Scroll speed

Tap frequency

Interaction rate

Session length

Example:

Fast scroll + low interaction = tired

Slow scroll + high interaction = engaged

3.2 Emotional State Detection



Based on:



Types of content they stop on

What they skip aggressively

Music preference at that moment



Example:



Skipping motivation → emotional overload

Watching calm → emotional fatigue

3.3 Time-of-Day Bias (Baseline Mood)

Time	Default Mood

Morning	Hopeful

Afternoon	Distracted

Evening	Social

Night	Vulnerable



Real behavior overrides baseline.



3.4 Mood State Model (Simple)

UserMood {

  Energy: High / Medium / Low

  Emotion: Positive / Neutral / Sensitive

  Intent: Relax / Compete / Escape

}



This updates every few minutes.



4️⃣ How to know when user is tired (Very Practical)



Tired users show escape behavior, not boredom.



Tired Signals:

Increasing scroll speed

Decreasing completion rate

Ignoring challenges

No sound / low volume

Late-night usage

Tired User Response (CRITICAL)



Do NOT:



Push motivation

Push competition



Do:



Micro-joy

Soft loops

Calm transitions

No decisions



This prevents burnout.



5️⃣ Zeigarnik Effect (Unfinished Loop Psychology)



🧠 Brain hates unfinished stories.



How to Use It (Ethically)

Content Level

Split story across 2 shorts

Pause before climax

“Wait for the end…”

Feed Level

“Next clip decides the winner”

“Result revealed next”

Challenge Level (Your App GOLD)

Progress bar not full

Near-miss outcomes

Partial unlocks

Rule:



Never delay closure too long, or users feel manipulated.



6️⃣ Emotional Wave Pattern (This is Advanced)



This is not random and not same for everyone.



Emotional Wave = Prevent Saturation



Humans cannot stay:



Happy too long

Emotional too long

Motivated too long

Default Wave Template

Light Joy

→ Relatable

→ Curiosity

→ Motivation

→ Neutral

→ Emotional

→ Light Joy



This resets emotional baseline.



Is it same for every user?

❌ No

Personal Emotional Signature



Each user has:



Emotion tolerance

Preferred peak emotion

Recovery speed



Example:



User A loves motivation

User B avoids emotional content



Your system learns:



UserEmotionTolerance[emotion_type]



Then:



Adjust wave length

Adjust peak intensity

Real-Time Adjustment



If user:



Skips emotional → shorten emotional segment

Engages deeply → allow deeper wave

Final Mental Model (Important)



You are NOT:



Ranking content



You ARE:



Regulating human nervous systems



The best feed feels like:



“This app gets me right now”

Perfect. Now we’ll engineer this properly, not conceptually.

I’ll give you (A) emotion-based feed ranking logic and (B) concrete data models for mood inference — in a way that fits your Go backend + Flutter frontend + challenge system.



Think of this as TikTok-level thinking, but founder-friendly.



A️⃣ Emotion-Based Feed Ranking (Core Algorithm)

0. Mental Model (Very Important)



You are not ranking posts

You are regulating user emotional state over time



So the ranking goal is NOT:



“Show best content”



It IS:



“Show the right emotion at the right moment”



1️⃣ Core Inputs to Feed Ranking



Every feed request uses 4 inputs:



FeedContext {

  UserMoodState

  EmotionalWavePosition

  UserEmotionPreferences

  ContentEmotionScores

}

2️⃣ Step 1: Define Emotion Types (Fixed Set)



Keep it small and stable:



enum EmotionType {

  JOY          // funny, cute, satisfying

  RELATABLE    // POV, daily life

  CURIOSITY    // mystery, unfinished

  MOTIVATION   // growth, wins

  EMOTIONAL    // touching, deep

  NEUTRAL      // filler, reset

}

3️⃣ Step 2: Content Emotion Profile



Each short has weights, not one label.



ContentEmotionProfile {

  content_id

  joy_score         // 0.0 – 1.0

  relatable_score

  curiosity_score

  motivation_score

  emotional_score

  neutral_score

}



These scores are learned from audience behavior, not creator tags.



4️⃣ Step 3: User Emotion Preference Profile



Each user has an emotional fingerprint:



UserEmotionPreference {

  user_id

  joy_affinity

  relatable_affinity

  curiosity_affinity

  motivation_affinity

  emotional_affinity

  fatigue_threshold   // how fast they burn out

}



Example:



User A: High motivation tolerance

User B: Avoids emotional content

5️⃣ Step 4: User Mood State (Real-Time)



(We’ll define data model fully in section B)



UserMoodState {

  energy_level   // HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW

  sensitivity    // NORMAL, SENSITIVE

  intent         // RELAX, COMPETE, ESCAPE

}

6️⃣ Step 5: Emotional Wave Controller



This is the brain of the system.



Emotional Wave State

UserEmotionalWave {

  current_phase

  last_emotion_shown

  intensity_level

}

Default Wave Template

[JOY → RELATABLE → CURIOSITY → MOTIVATION → NEUTRAL → EMOTIONAL → JOY]



But this adapts per user.



7️⃣ Step 6: Ranking Formula (Simple but Powerful)



For each candidate short:



FinalScore =

  BaseQualityScore

* EmotionMatchScore

* MoodCompatibilityScore

* WaveFitScore

* AntiBurnoutPenalty

7.1 EmotionMatchScore

EmotionMatchScore =

  Σ (ContentEmotionScore[e] * UserEmotionPreference[e])

7.2 MoodCompatibilityScore



Rules example:



If user.energy == LOW:

  boost JOY, NEUTRAL

  suppress MOTIVATION, EMOTIONAL

7.3 WaveFitScore

If content.emotion == next_expected_wave_emotion:

  WaveFitScore = 1.2

Else:

  WaveFitScore = 0.9



This creates flow instead of randomness.



7.4 Anti-Burnout Penalty

If same emotion shown > threshold:

  penalty += 0.3



This prevents emotional overload.



8️⃣ Final Feed Selection Logic

Determine user mood

Determine next emotional wave phase

Filter content compatible with mood

Rank using formula

Serve

Update wave state after interaction

B️⃣ Data Models for Mood Inference (Concrete & Implementable)



Now the hard part done simply.



1️⃣ Raw Interaction Events (Truth Layer)



Store EVERYTHING minimal:



UserInteractionEvent {

  user_id

  content_id

  event_type    // VIEW, LIKE, SKIP, PAUSE, REPLAY, SHARE

  watch_time

  timestamp

}



This is ground truth.



2️⃣ Session Snapshot Model



Updated every few minutes:



UserSessionState {

  user_id

  session_id

  avg_scroll_speed

  interaction_rate

  completion_rate

  session_duration

  time_of_day

}

3️⃣ Energy Level Inference

EnergyLevel =

  if scroll_speed high AND interaction low → LOW

  if scroll_speed medium AND interaction medium → MEDIUM

  if interaction high → HIGH



Store:



UserEnergyState {

  user_id

  energy_level

  confidence_score

  updated_at

}

4️⃣ Emotional Sensitivity Detection

If skips EMOTIONAL content fast

AND watches JOY content longer

→ sensitivity = HIGH

UserSensitivityState {

  user_id

  sensitivity_level  // NORMAL / SENSITIVE

}

If long passive scroll → ESCAPE

If likes/shares ↑ → RELAX

UserIntentState {

  user_id

  intent

This is what feed ranking reads:

  user_id

  energy_level

  sensitivity

  intent

  updated_at

}



Cache this in Redis for fast reads.

This updates UserEmotionPreference slowly.

## Summary of the ChatGPT Conversation

This is a **deep product/psychology design conversation** for your Battle Arena app. It covers how to make the feed engaging using behavioral psychology, not just code. Here's the breakdown:

---

### Topics Covered

**1. Feed Addiction Psychology (Ethical)**
- Users open apps for **emotional state change**, not content
- Dopamine = anticipation + reward prediction, not just pleasure
- **6 content emotion buckets**: Micro-Joy, Relatability, Motivation, Curiosity, Emotional Release, Status/Ego Boost
- Feed should rotate these in an **Emotional Wave Pattern** to prevent burnout

**2. Scroll Gesture Psychology**
- Vertical scroll = time dissolution (passive, relaxing)
- Horizontal swipe = decision/control (competitive, challenges)
- Tap/Hold = anticipation dopamine
- Rule: one gesture = one outcome, always

**3. Anti-Burnout Algorithm**
- Detect tired users via scroll speed, interaction rate, time-of-day
- Tired users get micro-joy/calm content; energetic users get competition
- Cap emotional intensity per session (max 2 emotional, 3 motivation pieces)
- Graceful exit prompts increase trust and retention

**4. Daily Emotional Journey Map**
- Morning → motivation/hope; Afternoon → light fun; Evening → social/ego; Night → comfort/calm
- Match feed emotion to real-world time-based mood

**5. Ethical Addiction vs Dark Patterns**
- Ethical = user feels better after using the app
- Dark = guilt, FOMO manipulation, punishing inactivity
- Test: "Does user feel better or worse after 20 minutes?"

**6. Emotion-Based Feed Ranking Algorithm**
- Content gets multi-emotion scores (joy, relatable, curiosity, etc.) learned from audience behavior
- Users get an **emotional fingerprint** (affinities + fatigue threshold)
- Real-time **UserMoodState** (energy, sensitivity, intent) inferred from behavior
- Ranking formula: `BaseQuality * EmotionMatch * MoodCompatibility * WaveFit * AntiBurnoutPenalty`
- Mood state cached in Redis for fast reads

**7. Mood Inference Data Models**
- Raw interaction events (view, like, skip, pause, replay, share)
- Session snapshots (scroll speed, interaction rate, completion rate)
- Energy/sensitivity/intent detection from behavioral signals
- Daily aggregation updates long-term user emotion preferences

**8. Challenge System Design (3 Archetypes)**
- **Type A — Observation/Skill**: "Find 10 ducks" — timed, score = speed
- **Type B — Creative**: "Who sketches better" — community votes decide winner
- **Type C — Achievement**: "Beat my score in X game" — proof-based, fraud-risk
- Universal lifecycle: `CREATE → ACCEPT → PERFORM → VERIFY → RESULT → REWARD`
- Spectator mode + predictions for extra engagement
- Near-miss psychology: "You were 1.2s behind. Rematch?"

**9. Zeigarnik Effect**
- Brain hates unfinished loops — use split stories, "result revealed next", partial progress bars
- Don't delay closure too long or it feels manipulative

---

### Key Takeaway

The conversation designs your app as a **human behavior regulation system**, not just a content feed. The core ideas are:
- Infer user mood silently from behavior
- Serve emotionally appropriate content in wave patterns
- Turn content into interactive play via challenges
- Keep users feeling **better** after each session

None of this has been implemented in the current codebase yet — the backend/frontend currently use basic chronological/mixed feeds with no emotion scoring, mood inference, or wave patterns.

now i want to know how social media app get notifications of everything like if someone send request and like someone profile or anyother thing.

Excellent question — this is a core part of any social media app, and since you’re already building a Flutter + Go + Node.js + Redis + MongoDB architecture, you’re in a great position to implement it cleanly.

Let’s break down how social media platforms (Instagram, Facebook, X, etc.) deliver real-time notifications — and how you can implement it step-by-step in your app.
🧩 1. The Core Concept

There are two layers of notifications:

    Backend event generation → something happens (like, follow, comment, etc.)

    Real-time delivery + storage → user gets notified on app (and optionally via push)

Every “action” in your app (like, comment, follow, challenge, DM) triggers an event that is handled by the notification system.
⚙️ 2. Architecture Overview

Here’s a clean architecture for notifications in your stack:
🔹 Event Source

When any of these happen:

    User A likes User B’s post

    User A follows User B

    User A sends a challenge

    User A comments on User B’s post

→ The service (Go or Node) emits an event message, like:

{
  "type": "like",
  "fromUser": "userA_id",
  "toUser": "userB_id",
  "postId": "post_id",
  "timestamp": 1731325400
}

🔹 Message Queue or Pub/Sub (optional but ideal)

Big apps use Redis Pub/Sub or Kafka/RabbitMQ so notifications can be processed asynchronously.

In your case (since you already have Redis):

    Node.js (or Go) publishes event → notifications_channel

    A Notification Service subscribes to it.

Example:

// Publisher (when like occurs)
redis.Publish(ctx, "notifications", eventJSON)

// Subscriber (notification service)
sub := redis.Subscribe(ctx, "notifications")
for msg := range sub.Channel() {
   handleNotification(msg.Payload)
}

🔹 Notification Storage (MongoDB)

Each user has a notifications collection:

{
  "_id": "notif_123",
  "user_id": "target_user_id",
  "type": "like",
  "from_user": "userA_id",
  "post_id": "post_id",
  "is_read": false,
  "created_at": "2025-11-12T18:30:00Z"
}

This allows you to:

    Display notification list in profile or bell icon.

    Filter unread vs read.

    Mark notifications as read.

🔹 Real-Time Delivery (WebSocket)

Since you’re already using a Node.js WebSocket server, it can:

    Listen for new notifications in Redis (via subscriber)

    Push to the target user instantly if they’re online.

Example flow:

User A likes → Go service publishes to Redis
→ Node WebSocket server subscribed to Redis
→ Sends JSON payload to User B’s socket connection
→ Flutter receives and shows real-time popup

Example WebSocket message:

{
  "event": "notification",
  "data": {
    "type": "like",
    "from_username": "userA",
    "post_id": "xyz"
  }
}

🔹 Push Notifications (Optional but common)

When the user is offline, you can send Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) push notification.

Your Node service can check:

if (!userIsOnline) {
   sendPushNotification(targetUser.fcmToken, "New like from @userA")
}

🔔 3. Flutter Frontend Flow

On Flutter side:

    Connect to WebSocket on login.

    Listen for "notification" events.

    Display a local notification (snackbar, toast, or bell badge).

    Store them locally or fetch via REST /api/notifications.

Example:

channel.stream.listen((message) {
  final data = jsonDecode(message);
  if (data['event'] == 'notification') {
    showNotification(data['data']);
  }
});

🧠 4. Summary of Responsibilities
Layer	Responsibility
Go or Node service	Generate event when action occurs
Redis	Publish/Subscribe for real-time distribution
Notification Service	Store in MongoDB + push via WebSocket
WebSocket Server	Deliver to online users instantly
FCM (optional)	Push to offline users
Flutter App	Listen & display notification
🪄 Example Notification Types
Event Type	Triggered When	Payload Example
like	A likes B’s post	{type:'like', from:'A', postId:'123'}
follow	A follows B	{type:'follow', from:'A'}
comment	A comments on B’s post	{type:'comment', from:'A', postId:'123'}
challenge	A challenges B	{type:'challenge', from:'A', league:'Gold'}

So, you’re building your entire backend in Go (Golang) and frontend in Flutter — no Node.js involved. That means you’ll handle notifications, real-time updates, and database operations all inside Go.

Let’s now design the complete Go-based notification system — similar to how big social apps (Instagram, TikTok) do it — but optimized for your current tech stack.
⚙️ OVERVIEW — How Notifications Work in Your Go + Flutter App

Here’s the data flow:

User A likes/follows/comments → Go backend generates event
→ Store in MongoDB (notifications collection)
→ Publish to Redis (for real-time)
→ WebSocket server (Go) pushes to User B if online
→ Flutter client receives live notification

If User B is offline, the next time they open the app, you fetch unread notifications via an API.
🧱 1. Database Design (MongoDB)

You’ll store notifications in a notifications collection.
Each document can look like this:

{
  "_id": "notif_101",
  "user_id": "target_user_id",       // who receives it
  "from_user_id": "actor_user_id",   // who triggered it
  "type": "like",                    // like | follow | comment | challenge
  "post_id": "optional_post_id",
  "is_read": false,
  "created_at": "2025-11-12T18:30:00Z"
}

🧩 2. Redis Pub/Sub (Real-Time Layer)

Since Redis is already part of your architecture, it’s perfect for broadcasting events between your Go microservices.
Example Go Code
Publisher (e.g., inside your Like or Follow handler)

event := map[string]interface{}{
    "type": "like",
    "from_user_id": fromUserID,
    "to_user_id": toUserID,
    "post_id": postID,
    "timestamp": time.Now().Unix(),
}
data, _ := json.Marshal(event)
redisClient.Publish(ctx, "notifications", data)

Subscriber (your WebSocket microservice)

sub := redisClient.Subscribe(ctx, "notifications")
ch := sub.Channel()

for msg := range ch {
    var event map[string]interface{}
    json.Unmarshal([]byte(msg.Payload), &event)

    // Find if the user is connected via WebSocket
    toUserID := event["to_user_id"].(string)
    if conn, ok := activeConnections[toUserID]; ok {
        conn.WriteJSON(event) // send real-time notification
    }

    // Store in MongoDB for persistence
    notificationsCollection.InsertOne(ctx, event)
}

🔌 3. WebSocket Server (Go)

This is the real-time bridge between your backend and Flutter app.
You’ll maintain a map of connected users:

var activeConnections = make(map[string]*websocket.Conn)

Connection Handler

func handleConnections(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    conn, _ := upgrader.Upgrade(w, r, nil)
    userID := r.URL.Query().Get("user_id") // Flutter sends user_id as query param
    activeConnections[userID] = conn

    defer func() {
        conn.Close()
        delete(activeConnections, userID)
    }()

    for {
        _, msg, err := conn.ReadMessage()
        if err != nil {
            break
        }
        fmt.Printf("Received message from user %s: %s\n", userID, msg)
    }
}

So now, when a Redis event arrives, you can instantly conn.WriteJSON() to the correct user.
📱 4. Flutter Client Side

In Flutter, you’ll connect using the web_socket_channel package.
Example:

import 'package:web_socket_channel/io.dart';
import 'dart:convert';

late IOWebSocketChannel channel;

void connectWebSocket(String userId) {
  channel = IOWebSocketChannel.connect(
    Uri.parse('ws://your-server-ip:8080/ws?user_id=$userId'),
  );

  channel.stream.listen((message) {
    final data = jsonDecode(message);
    if (data['type'] == 'like') {
      // Show local notification or snackbar
      print("New Like from ${data['from_user_id']}");
    }
  });
}

This allows instant notification delivery while the app is open.
📡 5. REST API for Notifications (for offline users)

When the user opens the app, Flutter should call an endpoint like:

GET /api/notifications?user_id=123

Go Handler

func GetNotifications(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    userID := r.URL.Query().Get("user_id")
    ctx := context.TODO()

    cur, err := notificationsCollection.Find(ctx, bson.M{"user_id": userID})
    if err != nil {
        http.Error(w, err.Error(), http.StatusInternalServerError)
        return
    }

    var notifs []bson.M
    cur.All(ctx, &notifs)
    json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(notifs)
}

🔔 6. Summary of Full Flow
Step	Action	Component
1	User likes/follows/comments	Go API
2	Publish event to Redis	Go API
3	Redis notifies WebSocket service	Go WebSocket
4	WebSocket pushes to online users	Go WebSocket + Flutter
5	MongoDB stores for persistence	Notification microservice
6	Flutter fetches offline notifications via API	REST endpoint
🚀 7. Optional Enhancements

    Unread counter → store unread count in Redis for quick display

    Mark as read API → /api/notifications/read

    Push notifications → integrate Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) later for offline alerts

    Notification batching → group similar events (“5 people liked your post”)

✅ Exactly right — you’ve got it perfectly.

Let’s walk through that logic clearly, step by step — this is exactly how modern social media platforms do it internally (Instagram, Twitter/X, TikTok, etc.), and it fits your Go + Flutter stack beautifully.
🧠 Concept in Simple Terms

Think of notifications as “records of actions” stored in your backend.

Whenever any user does something meaningful (like, follow, comment, challenge), your backend creates a record of that event — that’s the notification.

Then, you have two situations:
Situation	What Happens
Target user is online	WebSocket connection is active → send it instantly (real-time)
Target user is offline	Just store it in DB → send later when they come online or fetch via API
🔄 Step-by-Step Flow
🧩 1. An action happens

Example:
User A likes User B’s post.

Your Go API that handles likes will:

    Save the “like” itself (post_likes table)

    Create a notification record in MongoDB (or PostgreSQL)

    Publish a Redis message for real-time delivery.

notification := Notification{
    UserID:     targetUserID,    // who will receive
    FromUserID: actorUserID,     // who triggered
    Type:       "like",
    PostID:     postID,
    IsRead:     false,
    CreatedAt:  time.Now(),
}

// 1️⃣ Store in DB
notificationsCollection.InsertOne(ctx, notification)

// 2️⃣ Publish event
eventJSON, _ := json.Marshal(notification)
redisClient.Publish(ctx, "notifications", eventJSON)

🔌 2. WebSocket server is subscribed to Redis

Your Go WebSocket service listens to Redis:

sub := redisClient.Subscribe(ctx, "notifications")
ch := sub.Channel()

for msg := range ch {
    var notif Notification
    json.Unmarshal([]byte(msg.Payload), &notif)

    // check if user is connected
    if conn, ok := activeConnections[notif.UserID]; ok {
        conn.WriteJSON(notif)  // 🔥 instant delivery
    }
}

🧭 3. Online user receives instantly

On the Flutter side, if User B is currently using the app and connected to WebSocket:

channel.stream.listen((message) {
  final data = jsonDecode(message);
  print("🔥 New notification: ${data['type']} from ${data['from_user_id']}");
});

User B sees:

    “@UserA liked your post”

Instantly — no refresh, no polling.
💤 4. Offline user gets it later

If User B is not connected, the WebSocket check (activeConnections[userID]) fails.

But that’s fine — because you already stored the notification in MongoDB.

So when they open the app later, Flutter just calls:

GET /api/notifications?user_id=UserB

and the backend returns all unread notifications.
📱 5. User opens app → WebSocket reconnects

When User B opens the app, the Flutter app:

    Connects WebSocket (/ws?user_id=B)

    Fetches unread notifications (/api/notifications?user_id=B)

    Marks them as read if user opens them (PATCH /api/notifications/read)

🔐 6. Summary — What You Understood (and It’s 100% Correct)

✅ When someone performs an action → backend logs it (DB + Redis).
✅ If the target user is online (WebSocket active) → instantly push it.
✅ If offline → just keep it in DB → send next time via REST fetch or on reconnect.

This ensures no notification is lost, and users always get consistent behavior.

/notifications/
   model.go
   handler.go
   websocket.go
   redis.go

Explain it in a way like what happens when we click follow or like button in frontend how it send trigger event to backend and in backend how it handles and stores all those triggers and in what way how they send it to target user all that

Perfect — you want a real-world walkthrough of what exactly happens when a user taps “Follow” or “Like” in a social media app — from the moment the user clicks the button in the frontend, all the way to when the target user gets a notification.

Let’s go through it end-to-end, step by step, in your Go + Flutter architecture.
🚀 FULL FLOW: “User A follows User B”

Let’s say:
👉 User A (actor) clicks “Follow” on User B (target)

We’ll break this down into Frontend → API → Backend → Notification → Real-time delivery
🧩 1. FRONTEND (Flutter)
🖱 User Action

User A taps the Follow button in Flutter:

ElevatedButton(
  onPressed: () async {
    final response = await http.post(
      Uri.parse("http://yourserver.com/api/follow"),
      body: {
        "follower_id": currentUserId,
        "following_id": targetUserId
      },
    );
  },
  child: Text("Follow"),
)

So, Flutter simply sends a POST request to your Go backend with two IDs:

    follower_id: the one who clicked Follow (User A)

    following_id: the one being followed (User B)

⚙️ 2. BACKEND (Go API)

When /api/follow endpoint receives this POST:
Step 1️⃣ — Save the relationship in DB

In your PostgreSQL or MongoDB:

// Example structure
type Follow struct {
    FollowerID string `bson:"follower_id"`
    FollowingID string `bson:"following_id"`
    CreatedAt time.Time `bson:"created_at"`
}

follow := Follow{
    FollowerID: followerID,
    FollowingID: followingID,
    CreatedAt: time.Now(),
}
followsCollection.InsertOne(ctx, follow)

So now, the “Follow” relationship exists in your database.
Step 2️⃣ — Create a Notification Object

Immediately after saving, your backend creates a notification record for the target user (User B):

notif := Notification{
    UserID:     followingID,   // target (B)
    FromUserID: followerID,    // actor (A)
    Type:       "follow",
    IsRead:     false,
    CreatedAt:  time.Now(),
}
notificationsCollection.InsertOne(ctx, notif)

So now MongoDB has:

{
  "user_id": "UserB",
  "from_user_id": "UserA",
  "type": "follow",
  "is_read": false,
  "created_at": "2025-11-12T22:00:00Z"
}

Step 3️⃣ — Publish an Event to Redis

This lets the notification system or WebSocket service know something happened.

event, _ := json.Marshal(notif)
redisClient.Publish(ctx, "notifications", event)

That means you’re saying:

    “Hey, User A followed User B — whoever handles notifications, take care of it.”

⚙️ 3. BACKEND (Notification Handler / WebSocket Service)

You have a Go WebSocket service subscribed to Redis.
It’s always listening for new messages in the "notifications" channel.
Example:

sub := redisClient.Subscribe(ctx, "notifications")
ch := sub.Channel()

for msg := range ch {
    var notif Notification
    json.Unmarshal([]byte(msg.Payload), &notif)

    // Check if the target user (User B) is online
    if conn, ok := activeConnections[notif.UserID]; ok {
        // send instantly through websocket
        conn.WriteJSON(notif)
    } else {
        // if offline, do nothing (already saved in DB)
    }
}

⚡ 4. REAL-TIME DELIVERY (WebSocket)

If User B is currently using the app and has a WebSocket connection open, then your WebSocket server sends the notification instantly:

{
  "type": "follow",
  "from_user_id": "UserA",
  "message": "UserA started following you."
}

On the Flutter side, you already have a listener like:

channel.stream.listen((message) {
  final data = jsonDecode(message);
  if (data['type'] == 'follow') {
    showNotification("New follower!", "${data['from_user_id']} followed you");
  }
});

So User B instantly sees a popup or badge — real-time!
💤 5. IF USER IS OFFLINE

If User B is not connected to WebSocket, the backend doesn’t find their connection in activeConnections.

That’s fine — because:

    The notification is already saved in MongoDB

    When User B next opens the app, Flutter calls:

    GET /api/notifications?user_id=UserB

    The backend fetches all notifications:

    cur, _ := notificationsCollection.Find(ctx, bson.M{"user_id": userID})
    cur.All(ctx, &results)
    json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(results)

    Flutter shows them in the Notification screen.

❤️ LIKE EVENT — Similar Logic

When User A likes User B’s post:
Frontend

await http.post(
  Uri.parse("http://yourserver.com/api/like"),
  body: {
    "user_id": currentUserId,
    "post_id": postId
  },
);

Backend (Go)

    Save like record in post_likes

    Get the post’s owner (targetUserID)

    Create notification (type: "like")

    Publish to Redis (notifications channel)

    WebSocket server pushes it instantly if target is online

🔄 SUMMARY — Entire Chain of Events
Step	Component	What Happens
1	Flutter UI	User taps “Follow” or “Like”
2	Go API	Receives request, validates user
3	Go API	Saves action (follow/like) in DB
4	Go API	Creates notification record (MongoDB)
5	Go API	Publishes event to Redis
6	Go WebSocket	Subscribed to Redis, receives event
7	WebSocket Server	Sends to target user instantly if online
8	MongoDB	Persists all notifications
9	Flutter App	Listens to WebSocket or fetches via REST
10	User B sees notification	Real-time or next time they open app
📬 Real-world Analogy

It’s like a postal + live chat combo system:

    You (User A) send a “Follow letter”.

    The backend (post office) logs it and stores it in the target’s mailbox (DB).

    If the target (User B) is online (door open via WebSocket), the postman delivers immediately.

    If they’re offline, the letter just stays in their mailbox.

    When they open the app, they “check their mailbox” (GET /notifications).

Would you like me to show this flow as a diagram (visual) — arrows from Flutter → Go API → Redis → WebSocket → Flutter — so it’s easier to visualize the data movement?

So when multiple user send notification to simultaneously what happens 

Excellent — this is the right next question 👏

You’re now thinking about concurrency and scalability, which are exactly what make social apps reliable when thousands (or millions) of users generate notifications at the same time.

Let’s go step-by-step through what happens when many users trigger notifications simultaneously (e.g. 10 people follow or like someone at once) — and how your Go + Redis + MongoDB setup handles that safely and efficiently.
⚙️ 1. Multiple Users Trigger Actions at Once

Let’s say 10 different users (UserA1 → UserA10) all like posts belonging to UserB within the same second.

So, from the frontend:

    10 separate HTTP POST requests hit your /api/like endpoint almost simultaneously.

In Go, each request runs in its own goroutine — Go’s built-in concurrency system.

func LikePostHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    go processLike(r) // handled concurrently
}

Each goroutine does:

    Saves the like in DB

    Creates a notification record

    Publishes an event to Redis

Go’s goroutines are lightweight — they can handle thousands of concurrent events easily.
🧩 2. Redis Pub/Sub Handles Many Messages in Parallel

Each of those goroutines calls:

redisClient.Publish(ctx, "notifications", eventJSON)

Redis queues all these messages and delivers them (in order) to every subscriber — like your WebSocket service.

Redis is extremely fast (handles millions of messages per second), so even if 10,000 notifications arrive in one second, they’re processed sequentially but instantly.
⚡ 3. WebSocket Service (Subscriber) Receives a Stream of Events

Your WebSocket service is subscribed to Redis and has a single listener loop:

sub := redisClient.Subscribe(ctx, "notifications")
ch := sub.Channel()

for msg := range ch {
    go handleNotification(msg.Payload) // each message handled in its own goroutine
}

Each handleNotification() runs in its own goroutine — again, fully concurrent — so 10+ messages can be processed simultaneously.

This function:

    Parses the message

    Saves it in MongoDB (if not already stored)

    Checks if the user is online (activeConnections)

    If yes → sends via conn.WriteJSON()

💾 4. MongoDB Stores Them Separately

Each incoming event is inserted as a new document in the notifications collection.

Example (10 notifications for UserB):

[
  {"from_user": "A1", "to_user": "B", "type": "like"},
  {"from_user": "A2", "to_user": "B", "type": "like"},
  ...
  {"from_user": "A10", "to_user": "B", "type": "like"}
]

MongoDB can handle multiple writes in parallel — especially if you’re using one shared connection pool.
Each write is atomic, so no two notifications overwrite each other.
🔌 5. WebSocket Pushes Them Out — Instantly and Safely

If UserB is online and has a WebSocket connection open:

if conn, ok := activeConnections[userID]; ok {
    conn.WriteJSON(event)
}

Multiple goroutines might call WriteJSON to the same connection nearly at the same time — so you usually wrap this in a mutex lock to avoid race conditions:

var connMutex sync.Mutex

connMutex.Lock()
conn.WriteJSON(event)
connMutex.Unlock()

That ensures each notification is sent one after another safely.
📨 6. Flutter App Receives Notifications in Sequence

The Flutter app’s WebSocket listener gets multiple messages back-to-back:

channel.stream.listen((message) {
  final data = jsonDecode(message);
  print("New notification: ${data['from_user_id']} ${data['type']}");
});

If 10 likes happen, it receives 10 separate messages — near real-time (milliseconds apart).

You can choose to:

    Show each individually (“@userA liked your post”)

    Or group them later (“10 people liked your post”)

🧠 7. Key Takeaways (Concurrency Safety)
Step	How it Handles Simultaneous Events
Go API	Goroutines allow thousands of concurrent actions
Redis Pub/Sub	Buffers + broadcasts all messages quickly
WebSocket Service	Handles messages concurrently (via goroutines)
MongoDB	Each insert is atomic and concurrent-safe
WebSocket Delivery	Mutex around connection write avoids collision
Flutter App	Processes incoming events as a stream (sequentially)
🔒 8. Optional Scaling Techniques (for Future Growth)

If your app grows big:

    Use a Redis Stream (instead of Pub/Sub) → guaranteed delivery, replayable messages

    Add a notification microservice that only handles notification creation

    Use message batching (“5 new likes”) to reduce spam

    Add a rate limiter to prevent abuse

    Use load balancer + multiple WebSocket servers (with Redis for shared state)

In short:

    Even when 1,000 people like or follow someone at once, Go handles each action in parallel, Redis queues the events, MongoDB saves them individually, and WebSocket delivers them instantly or later when the user is online — all safely and efficiently.

Would you like me to show you a visual flow diagram of this “multi-user simultaneous notification” process (from frontend clicks → concurrent Go handlers → Redis → WebSocket → Flutter)? It’ll make this whole flow super clear.

So what is websocket or what is http api call how it connects. Does it with websocket or rest api

Excellent question 👏 — this is a key concept in how social media apps handle real-time notifications and user interactions.
Let’s break it down step-by-step, especially in your case (Flutter frontend + Go backend).
🧩 1. What’s the difference between HTTP API and WebSocket?
Concept	HTTP API (REST)	WebSocket
Connection Type	One request → one response. Then connection closes.	Persistent, continuous two-way connection.
Direction	Client → Server only (server replies once).	Both Client ↔ Server (real-time).
Use Case	Signup, login, like button, follow button, fetching feed, etc.	Notifications, messages, live updates, typing indicators, etc.
Analogy	Like making a phone call, saying one sentence, and hanging up.	Like staying on a call continuously to talk both ways.
⚙️ 2. What happens when you click “Follow” or “Like” (with Go + Flutter)

Let’s go step-by-step:
Step 1 – Flutter sends REST API call

When user clicks the Follow button:

await http.post(
  Uri.parse("https://api.yourapp.com/follow"),
  body: {
    "follower_id": currentUserId,
    "following_id": targetUserId,
  },
);

So Flutter → sends a POST request to Go backend (via HTTP API).
Step 2 – Go Backend handles the request

Your Go server has a REST endpoint, for example:

func FollowHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    // Parse data
    followerID := r.FormValue("follower_id")
    followingID := r.FormValue("following_id")

    // Store in database
    _, err := db.Exec(`INSERT INTO follows (follower_id, following_id) VALUES ($1, $2)`, followerID, followingID)
    if err != nil {
        http.Error(w, err.Error(), 500)
        return
    }

    // Create a notification
    _, err = db.Exec(`INSERT INTO notifications (user_id, type, from_user, created_at) VALUES ($1, $2, $3, NOW())`,
        followingID, "follow", followerID)
}

✅ It does two things:

    Stores the follow in the follows table.

    Creates a notification entry in notifications table.

Step 3 – Notify target user (via WebSocket)

If the target user (the one being followed) is online — i.e., they have a WebSocket connection open — then you can instantly send them a real-time notification.

You’ll maintain a map of online users in your Go WebSocket server:

var clients = make(map[string]*websocket.Conn)

When you create the notification:

if conn, ok := clients[followingID]; ok {
    // user is online
    message := map[string]string{
        "type": "notification",
        "event": "follow",
        "from_user": followerID,
    }
    conn.WriteJSON(message)
}

✅ This pushes a live notification to the Flutter app instantly.
Step 4 – Flutter receives real-time message

In Flutter, your WebSocket listener will receive:

channel.stream.listen((message) {
  var data = jsonDecode(message);
  if (data['type'] == 'notification') {
    // show snackbar, update bell icon count, etc.
  }
});

Now the user instantly sees:

    “@alex followed you”

without reloading or refreshing anything.
⚡ When multiple users send notifications simultaneously

Each notification is:

    Stored separately in the notifications table.

    Sent to the user’s WebSocket connection (if active).

    If not online → stays in DB → delivered when user reconnects.

So if 100 users like your photo at once:

    100 API calls are made to the backend.

    Each creates 1 DB entry.

    Each triggers conn.WriteJSON() to you (if you’re online).

    If you’re offline, you’ll see all 100 when you open the app next (via /api/get-notifications).

🧠 Summary
Step	Technology	Role
1️⃣	REST API (HTTP)	Send user action to backend (like/follow/comment)
2️⃣	Database	Save action + create notification record
3️⃣	WebSocket	Instantly deliver live update to target user
4️⃣	REST API (backup)	Fetch missed notifications when offline
