So why don't we use websocket if it constantly created connection rather than rest api. How does it connect with database does it do with rest api or websocket or is there any other method. And tell me if there is any other method for communication like restapi or websocket or etc.

Excellent — that’s a very deep backend engineering question 👍
Let’s go step-by-step and really clear this up properly.
🧠 Why we don’t use only WebSockets for everything

At first glance, it seems logical:

    “If WebSocket can send and receive continuously, why not use it for all communication?”

Here’s why we don’t 👇
Reason	Explanation
1. WebSocket is persistent	Every open WebSocket keeps a connection alive between client ↔ server. If you have 1 million users, that’s 1 million open sockets consuming memory and CPU. We don’t need that for signup/login/fetch feed — those are one-time operations.
2. REST API is simpler and cheaper for one-shot actions	Things like “login”, “create post”, “get profile” happen rarely and don’t need a continuous stream. A single HTTP request is lighter.
3. Security & scalability	Load balancers, caches (Cloudflare, Nginx, etc.), and proxies handle HTTP easily; WebSockets need special routing and are harder to scale.
4. Reliability	If the user loses connection, the WebSocket must reconnect, re-authenticate, and resync — more complexity. REST is stateless and simpler.

So the usual pattern:

    ✅ REST API → for regular actions (CRUD, authentication, uploading, etc.)

    ⚡ WebSocket → for real-time updates (notifications, chat, live likes)

    🗃️ Database → stores all state changes permanently

🔄 How database fits into this

Both REST and WebSocket routes talk to the same backend logic and database — the difference is how data is delivered.
REST API → Database flow

Flutter → HTTP POST → Go API → SQL (insert/update/select)

Example:

func CreatePost(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    db.Exec("INSERT INTO posts (user_id, caption) VALUES ($1, $2)", userID, caption)
}

WebSocket → Database flow

If the data comes via a WebSocket message (for example, in-app chat):

Flutter → WS message → Go WebSocket server → SQL (insert) + broadcast to others

Example:

func handleMessage(conn *websocket.Conn, message []byte) {
    var msg ChatMessage
    json.Unmarshal(message, &msg)
    db.Exec("INSERT INTO messages (sender_id, receiver_id, text) VALUES ($1,$2,$3)",
        msg.SenderID, msg.ReceiverID, msg.Text)

    // Then broadcast via WebSocket
    if targetConn, ok := clients[msg.ReceiverID]; ok {
        targetConn.WriteJSON(msg)
    }
}

So, both REST and WebSocket ultimately connect to the same database — just via different handlers.
🛰️ Other communication methods used in social apps

There are a few more you should know about 👇
Method	Direction	Real-time?	Typical Use
HTTP REST API	Client → Server	❌ No	Normal requests (login, fetch feed, post upload)
WebSocket	Client ↔ Server	✅ Yes	Chat, notifications, live events
gRPC (over HTTP/2)	Client ↔ Server	✅ Yes	High-performance internal service communication (microservices)
Server-Sent Events (SSE)	Server → Client (one-way)	✅ Yes	Real-time data stream (e.g., live feed updates)
MQTT	Client ↔ Broker	✅ Yes	Lightweight IoT or messaging apps (Telegram, WhatsApp use it)
GraphQL (over HTTP or WS)	Client ↔ Server	Optional	Flexible API queries
WebRTC	Peer ↔ Peer	✅ Yes	Voice/video calls, live streams
Push Notifications (FCM/APNs)	Server → Device OS	✅ Yes (but external)	Notify offline users via phone OS
⚙️ In your case (Go + Flutter)

Here’s how you’ll likely combine them:
Action	Communication	Description
Signup/Login	REST API	One-time HTTP call to authenticate user.
Follow / Like / Comment	REST API	Store in DB + trigger WebSocket notification to receiver.
Notifications / Chat	WebSocket	Live delivery to online users.
Offline users	REST API	Fetch notifications missed while offline.
Future (optional)	FCM Push	Send “You got a new follower” even when app closed.
🔑 In summary

    REST API → stateless, short, simple.

    WebSocket → continuous, bi-directional, real-time.

    Database → the single source of truth.

    Push / Queues / Events → make it scalable when many actions happen.

If you want, I can show you an architecture diagram that visually connects all this — from Flutter button click → Go REST → DB → WebSocket push → Flutter notification.
Would you like me to draw that out?

Where fcm push is used 

Perfect question 🔥 — because FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging) is the piece that connects your backend to the user’s device when the app isn’t running or the user is offline.

Let’s go step-by-step and tie it to your Go + Flutter social app 👇
📱 What is FCM?

Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) is a push notification service by Google.
It lets your backend server send notifications directly to users’ phones — even if:

    the app is closed,

    the phone is locked, or

    the user has no active WebSocket connection.

⚙️ How it fits into your social app flow

Let’s see the full lifecycle — from the moment someone performs an action.
🔹 1. User performs an action (e.g., “follow”)

Frontend (Flutter):

await http.post(
  Uri.parse("https://api.yourapp.com/follow"),
  body: {
    "follower_id": currentUserId,
    "following_id": targetUserId,
  },
);

🔹 2. Go backend handles it

Your Go backend receives the request:

func FollowHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    followerID := r.FormValue("follower_id")
    followingID := r.FormValue("following_id")

    // Save follow in DB
    db.Exec(`INSERT INTO follows (follower_id, following_id) VALUES ($1,$2)`, followerID, followingID)

    // Create notification in DB
    db.Exec(`INSERT INTO notifications (user_id, type, from_user, created_at) VALUES ($1,$2,$3,NOW())`,
        followingID, "follow", followerID)

    // Trigger FCM Push (for offline users)
    go sendFCMNotification(followingID, fmt.Sprintf("%s started following you", followerID))
}

🔹 3. Backend calls FCM

In your Go backend, you’ll use the Firebase Admin SDK or an HTTP POST to the FCM API:

func sendFCMNotification(targetUserID, message string) {
    token := getFCMTokenFromDB(targetUserID) // stored when user logged in

    fcmMessage := map[string]interface{}{
        "to": token,
        "notification": map[string]string{
            "title": "New Follower",
            "body":  message,
        },
    }

    // Send POST to FCM server
    http.Post("https://fcm.googleapis.com/fcm/send", "application/json", bytes.NewBuffer(jsonData))
}

✅ This will cause Google’s FCM servers to deliver the message to the user’s phone.
🔹 4. Flutter receives push notification (via OS)

Even if the app is closed, Android/iOS shows a push banner:

    🔔 “@alex started following you”

When the user taps it → Flutter app opens → you fetch all notifications via REST or WebSocket.
🧩 When FCM is used vs when WebSocket is used
Case	WebSocket	FCM
App is open & connected	✅ Real-time message via WebSocket	❌ Not used
App is backgrounded (running but not visible)	⚠️ Sometimes active	✅ Used to wake app / show banner
App is closed or phone locked	❌ WebSocket disconnected	✅ Used
Internet is off	❌ Can’t deliver	⚠️ FCM retries later
🧠 Where FCM fits in your architecture

[Flutter app]
     ↑
  WebSocket (real-time when open)
     ↑
[Go backend] —> Database
     │
     └──> FCM Push (via Google servers)
               ↓
           [User’s phone OS]

So FCM isn’t an alternative to WebSocket or REST —
👉 it’s a backup channel for offline or background notification delivery.
🗃️ How you manage it

Every user’s fcm_token is stored in your DB:

ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN fcm_token TEXT;

When they log in, Flutter registers it:

String? token = await FirebaseMessaging.instance.getToken();
await http.post(
  Uri.parse("https://api.yourapp.com/update-token"),
  body: {"user_id": userId, "fcm_token": token},
);

Then your Go backend can look it up anytime to push notifications.
✅ Summary
Component	Role
REST API	For one-time actions like follow/like/comment
WebSocket	Real-time updates when users are online
FCM Push	Notify offline users via Android/iOS
Database	Stores all notifications permanently

If you want, I can show you how to integrate FCM push with your Go backend (with Firebase Admin SDK setup and example code).
Would you like that next?

What fcm token represent and how user get notification when he is offline 

Perfect question again 💡 — this shows you’re thinking like a backend architect now.
Let’s break this down in simple but complete terms — so you fully understand what an FCM token is, how it works, and how offline notifications reach users.
🔑 What is an FCM token?

Think of the FCM token as your app’s unique address on the Firebase Cloud Messaging network.

    Every installation of your Flutter app on a phone has its own unique FCM token.

    It’s like a temporary ID that Google/Firebase assigns to that specific device + app instance.

    When you (the backend) want to send a notification to a user, you don’t talk to the phone directly — you tell Firebase:

        “Hey Firebase, deliver this message to the device with this token.”

Then Firebase finds that device and delivers the push.
📱 Analogy: Think of FCM tokens like WhatsApp phone numbers
Concept	Analogy
FCM token	Your WhatsApp phone number
FCM server	WhatsApp’s central server
Backend (your Go app)	Someone sending you a message
Device	Your phone

So if your backend says:

    Send a message to token f02a93b5:APA91b...

Firebase knows exactly which device and which app instance to deliver that notification to.
⚙️ How the FCM token is created (in Flutter)

When your app first starts, the Firebase SDK automatically generates it:

import 'package:firebase_messaging/firebase_messaging.dart';

void initFCM() async {
  String? token = await FirebaseMessaging.instance.getToken();
  print("User FCM Token: $token");
}

Then you send it to your backend:

await http.post(
  Uri.parse("https://api.yourapp.com/update-fcm-token"),
  body: {
    "user_id": userId,
    "fcm_token": token,
  },
);

🗄️ How you store it in your backend (Go + PostgreSQL)

You add a column:

ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN fcm_token TEXT;

When a user logs in:

func UpdateFCMToken(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    userID := r.FormValue("user_id")
    token := r.FormValue("fcm_token")

    _, err := db.Exec(`UPDATE users SET fcm_token=$1 WHERE id=$2`, token, userID)
    if err != nil {
        http.Error(w, err.Error(), 500)
        return
    }

    fmt.Fprint(w, "Token updated successfully")
}

Now your backend knows exactly which device belongs to which user.
🌐 What happens when the user is offline

Let’s walk through it clearly 👇
🧩 Step 1 – Backend triggers FCM

Someone performs an action — say Alex follows John.

Your backend inserts the “follow” in the database, and then looks up John’s FCM token:

token := getUserFCMToken("john_id")
sendFCMNotification(token, "Alex started following you!")

🧩 Step 2 – Your Go backend sends the notification to Firebase

fcmMessage := map[string]interface{}{
    "to": token,
    "notification": map[string]string{
        "title": "New Follower",
        "body":  "Alex started following you!",
    },
}

Firebase receives this message and queues it in its system.
🧩 Step 3 – Firebase delivers it to the OS (Android/iOS)

Even though John’s app is closed or offline, the Firebase background service (built into Android or iOS) is always running in the OS level.

Firebase keeps a secure connection with the device and holds the notification until:

    the phone reconnects to the internet,

    and the device’s FCM service receives the message.

Then it shows it as a push banner (like WhatsApp or Instagram do).
🧩 Step 4 – User taps the notification

When the user taps it:

    The app opens (even if it was closed)

    You can fetch new notifications via your /api/get-notifications endpoint

    You can clear the badge or show an in-app alert

🧠 Summary
Concept	Description
FCM Token	Unique ID for your app instance on that device.
Who issues it	Firebase SDK automatically.
Where it’s stored	Your database (users.fcm_token).
When used	When backend sends push via Firebase to that user.
Offline behavior	Firebase holds message → delivers when user reconnects.
When refreshed	Token may change (reinstall, clear data, logout) — so update backend.
⚡ Typical flow in your social app

(1) User installs app → gets FCM token
(2) Flutter → sends token to Go backend
(3) Go backend stores it in DB
(4) When another user likes/follows/comments
      → backend sends push via FCM
(5) Firebase delivers to user’s phone (even if app closed)

🔐 One last key thing

    FCM tokens can expire or rotate. So your Flutter app should refresh token periodically:

FirebaseMessaging.instance.onTokenRefresh.listen((newToken) {
  updateTokenToBackend(newToken);
});

Would you like me to show you a complete Go function that sends an FCM push to a user (using Firebase Admin SDK), so you can plug it into your backend directly?

What else i can use for push notification like apn 

Fantastic follow-up 💡 — yes, FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging) isn’t the only way to deliver push notifications. It’s just the most convenient cross-platform one.

Let’s break down what other options exist — and when you’d use each — especially for a Go + Flutter social app like yours.
🚀 1. FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging)

✅ You already know this one.

    Platform: Android, iOS, Web

    Who owns it: Google

    How it works: You send a message → Google’s FCM servers → user’s device.

    Pros:

        Free

        Works on both Android & iOS (through Apple APNs internally)

        Easy Flutter SDK

    Cons:

        Requires Google services (not ideal for China)

        Slight latency in delivery at times

🍎 2. APNs (Apple Push Notification service)

    Platform: iOS, macOS, watchOS

    Who owns it: Apple

    How it works:
    Your server connects directly to Apple’s APNs endpoint via HTTP/2 with a JWT or certificate, and Apple delivers it to iPhones.

    Pros:

        Official Apple service → best reliability on iOS

        Fine-grained control (mutable content, rich notifications, etc.)

    Cons:

        iOS only

        Requires Apple developer credentials

        More setup (certificates or tokens)

    Note: FCM on iOS actually uses APNs behind the scenes.
    When you send a message through FCM, Google routes it to APNs for delivery.

So if you already use FCM → you’re indirectly using APNs too for iPhones.
☁️ 3. OneSignal

    Type: Third-party push notification service

    Platforms: Android, iOS, Web, Flutter

    How it works: You integrate OneSignal SDK → OneSignal uses FCM/APNs internally.

    Pros:

        Simple integration

        Analytics, targeting, segments, A/B testing

    Cons:

        Vendor-lock (you rely on their dashboard)

        Paid for large volumes

Good for small to mid-size apps where you want dashboards & analytics easily.
📨 4. Amazon SNS (Simple Notification Service)

    Platform: Cross-platform (uses FCM, APNs, ADM, etc.)

    How it works: Unified interface for multiple push providers.

    Pros:

        Enterprise-grade, scalable

        Works with FCM, APNs, Amazon Device Messaging, and email/SMS

    Cons:

        More setup and cost

        Complex IAM permissions

🧩 5. Web Push / VAPID

    Platform: Web browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari)

    Use case: Progressive Web Apps (PWA)

    Pros: Doesn’t need mobile SDKs

    Cons: Only for browsers, not native Flutter apps

💬 6. Self-hosted brokers (for advanced setups)

If you want to run everything yourself, you can use:

    MQTT brokers (like Mosquitto, EMQX, or HiveMQ)
    → Used in WhatsApp-like architectures.

    Web Push Gateways (self-built over WebSockets)
    → Real-time in-app messaging, not OS-level notifications.

But:

    MQTT/WebSocket notifications only work while app is open.
    They can’t wake a closed app like FCM/APNs.

🧠 Summary table
Service	Platform	Real-time (when app open)	Offline Delivery	Best For
FCM	Android + iOS + Web	✅	✅	General use (your app)
APNs	iOS only	✅	✅	Native iOS apps
OneSignal	All	✅	✅	Easy setup, analytics
Amazon SNS	All	✅	✅	Enterprise apps
Web Push	Browser only	✅	✅	PWAs / websites
MQTT / WebSocket	All	✅	❌	In-app live messages only
⚙️ For your Go + Flutter app

You can choose between two main options:
Option A – FCM only (Recommended)

    Flutter SDK ready.

    Works for both Android and iOS.

    Your Go backend just sends one type of push.

Option B – Dual stack (for more control)

    Send directly to APNs for iOS users.

    Send to FCM for Android users.

    Your database stores which platform each user uses:

    users(id, platform, fcm_token, apns_token)

    Then your Go backend chooses the right service per user.

If you want, I can show you a Go example of sending push notifications via APNs directly (without FCM) so you can see how that works under the hood.
Would you like me to show that next?

package main

import (
	"encoding/json"
	"net/http"
	"sort"
	"strings"
)

// scoredUser is a helper struct just for sorting users with their search score.
// It is not exported and only used within this file.
type scoredUser struct {
	User  User
	Score float64
}

// SearchHandler handles requests to the /search endpoint.
func SearchHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
	query := r.URL.Query().Get("q")

	if query == "" {
		http.Error(w, "Missing search query parameter 'q'", http.StatusBadRequest)
		return
	}

	var scoredUsers []scoredUser

	// Use the global 'users' slice from database.go
	for _, user := range users {
		score := calculateScore(user, query)
		if score > 0 {
			scoredUsers = append(scoredUsers, scoredUser{User: user, Score: score})
		}
	}

	// Sort users based on their score in descending order.
	sort.Slice(scoredUsers, func(i, j int) bool {
		return scoredUsers[i].Score > scoredUsers[j].Score
	})

	// Extract the User objects from the sorted list for the response.
	resultUsers := make([]User, len(scoredUsers))
	for i, su := range scoredUsers {
		resultUsers[i] = su.User
	}

	// The SearchResponse struct is defined in models.go
	response := SearchResponse{
		Results: resultUsers,
		Total:   len(resultUsers),
	}

	w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
	json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(response)
}

// calculateScore calculates a relevance score for a user based on a search query.
// It now uses the FullName field and no longer considers location.
func calculateScore(user User, query string) float64 {
	var score float64
	query = strings.ToLower(query)

	// Strong match for username
	if strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(user.Username), query) {
		score += 10.0
	}
	
	// Weaker match for full name
	if strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(user.FullName), query) {
		score += 5.0
	}

	// Add a small bonus for having more followers
	score += float64(user.Followers) * 0.01 // Reduced follower bonus to avoid dominating score

	return score
}
search.go


package main

import (
	"encoding/json"
	"log"
	"net/http"

	"github.com/gorilla/mux"
)

// LoginHandler validates credentials and returns the user's data and all other users.
func LoginHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
	var creds struct {
		Username string json:"username"
		Password string json:"password"
	}

	if err := json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&creds); err != nil {
		http.Error(w, "Invalid request body", http.StatusBadRequest)
		return
	}

	// First, validate the user's credentials.
	if !IsValidUser(creds.Username, creds.Password) {
		http.Error(w, "Invalid username or password", http.StatusUnauthorized)
		log.Printf("Failed login attempt for user %s.", creds.Username)
		return
	}

	// If valid, fetch the logged-in user's full profile.
	user, exists := GetUserByUsername(creds.Username)
	if !exists {
		// This should not happen if IsValidUser passed, but it's good practice to check.
		http.Error(w, "Could not find user data after successful login", http.StatusInternalServerError)
		return
	}

	// Fetch all users for the search/discovery feature.
	allUsers := GetAllUsers()

	// Create the response payload that the Flutter client expects.
	response := map[string]interface{}{
		"user":     user,
		"allUsers": allUsers,
	}

	// Send the successful response.
	w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
	w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)
	json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(response)
	log.Printf("User %s logged in successfully. Sent user profile and all users list.", creds.Username)
}

// GetAllUsersHandler returns a list of all users.
func GetAllUsersHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
	w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
	allUsers := GetAllUsers()
	if err := json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(allUsers); err != nil {
		http.Error(w, "Failed to encode users", http.StatusInternalServerError)
	}
}

// GetUserHandler returns the data for a single user.
func GetUserHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
	vars := mux.Vars(r)
	username := vars["username"]

	user, exists := GetUserByUsername(username)
	if !exists {
		http.Error(w, "User not found", http.StatusNotFound)
		return
	}

	w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
	if err := json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(user); err != nil {
		http.Error(w, "Failed to encode user data", http.StatusInternalServerError)
	}
}

// main is the entry point for the application.
func main() {
	InitDatabase()

	r := mux.NewRouter()

	api := r.PathPrefix("/api/v1").Subrouter()
	api.HandleFunc("/users", GetAllUsersHandler).Methods("GET")
	api.HandleFunc("/users/{username}", GetUserHandler).Methods("GET")

	r.HandleFunc("/login", LoginHandler).Methods("POST")
	r.HandleFunc("/ws/{username}", WebsocketHandler).Methods("GET")

	log.Println("Starting server on :8081...")
	if err := http.ListenAndServe(":8081", r); err != nil {
		log.Fatalf("Could not start server: %s\n", err)
	}
}

main.go

Poor Scalability (This is the biggest issue): The algorithm checks every single user in your database for every single search. If you have 100,000 users, it has to perform 100,000 checks every time someone types a letter in the search bar. This will become very slow and expensive as your user base grows.
No Partial Word Matching: It searches for the exact text you type. If you search for "player one", it will not find a user named "The First Player". It doesn't understand that "player" and "one" are separate words.
Basic Relevance: The scoring is quite rigid. It doesn't handle typos or recognize that a shorter username that is a perfect match (e.g., searching "player1" and finding user "player1") is more relevant than a longer username that just contains the query (e.g., finding "anotherplayer123").

what approach should i use to overcome these cons.

Great question — you’ve reached the exact point where real social apps and search systems evolve.
I’ll explain this step-by-step, tied directly to your Go + Flutter backend, and then give you practical approaches you can implement gradually (not theoretical).
First: Why your current search has those problems (short recap)

Your current approach:

for _, user := range users {
    score := calculateScore(user, query)
}

Problems:

    O(N) scan → checks every user → bad at scale

    String contains only → no tokenization, no fuzzy match

    Static scoring → no typo tolerance, no intent understanding

This is fine for learning, but no real app stops here.
How REAL social apps solve this (progressive layers)

Social apps don’t jump directly to Elasticsearch. They evolve in phases.

I’ll give you 4 levels, from simplest → production-grade.
LEVEL 1 — Database-side filtering (Immediate improvement)
Key idea

👉 Move filtering from Go loop → database query

Instead of:

GetAllUsers() → loop in Go

Do:

SELECT * FROM users
WHERE username ILIKE '%query%'
   OR full_name ILIKE '%query%'
LIMIT 50;

Why this helps

    Database uses indexes

    Reduces scanned rows massively

    Faster than Go memory scan

PostgreSQL index (IMPORTANT)

CREATE INDEX idx_users_username ON users USING gin (username gin_trgm_ops);
CREATE INDEX idx_users_fullname ON users USING gin (full_name gin_trgm_ops);

Enable extension:

CREATE EXTENSION pg_trgm;

What this solves

✅ Scalability (big win)
❌ Still weak typo handling
❌ Still basic relevance
LEVEL 2 — Tokenization + weighted scoring (Still no search engine)

Instead of searching full query as one string:
Break query into words

tokens := strings.Fields(strings.ToLower(query))

Example:

"player one" → ["player", "one"]

Score per token

for _, token := range tokens {
    if strings.Contains(username, token) {
        score += 10
    }
    if strings.Contains(fullname, token) {
        score += 5
    }
}

Bonus logic

if username == query {
    score += 50 // exact match
}

Result

    "player one" matches "The First Player"

    Short usernames rank higher

    Still simple, but MUCH better UX

✅ Partial word matching
✅ Better relevance
❌ Still no typo tolerance
LEVEL 3 — Fuzzy matching (Typos) WITHOUT external engines
Use Levenshtein Distance

Example:

func similarity(a, b string) int {
    return levenshtein.Distance(a, b)
}

Then:

distance := levenshtein.Distance(username, query)
if distance <= 2 {
    score += 8
}

What this enables

    "plyaer1" → finds "player1"

    "jon" → finds "john"

⚠️ Use carefully:

    Only for top 50–100 DB results

    NOT on full user table

✅ Typo tolerance
❌ Still not web-scale
LEVEL 4 — Real-world scalable solution (Used by Instagram, Twitter)

At scale, search ≠ database
Architecture

Flutter
  ↓
Search API (Go)
  ↓
Search Engine (Elasticsearch / OpenSearch / Meilisearch)
  ↓
Postgres (source of truth)

Why?

Because search engines provide:

    Inverted indexes

    Fuzzy matching

    Ranking models

    Prefix search

    Fast autocomplete

Recommended search engines (Pick ONE)
🔹 Meilisearch (BEST for you)

    Very easy

    REST API

    Typo-tolerant by default

    Ranking rules configurable

🔹 OpenSearch / Elasticsearch

    Powerful

    More complex

    Heavy ops

How real apps sync data
When user updates profile

1. Save to PostgreSQL
2. Update search index

Example:

func UpdateUser(user User) {
    SaveToDB(user)
    SearchIndex.Upsert(user)
}

Search ranking formula (Production-style)

Final Score =
  Text relevance (username > fullname > bio)
+ Popularity (followers, wins)
+ Interaction signals (search clicks, profile opens)
+ Social proximity (mutual follows)

This is exactly what Instagram does.
Your BEST path forward (Very important)
Step-by-step roadmap for YOU

Now
✅ DB-side search + trigram index
✅ Tokenized scoring
✅ Limit results

Next
✅ Fuzzy match on top results

Later
✅ Meilisearch integration
Example: Final recommended Go search flow

1. Flutter sends query
2. Go API:
   - Query DB with ILIKE + LIMIT 100
   - Tokenize + score
   - Apply fuzzy matching
   - Sort + return top 20

Why this matches your current app

    Works with Go

    Works with Flutter

    No Node.js

    No Redis required

    Scales gradually

now i want to know how social media app get notifications of everything like if someone send request and like someone profile or anyother thing.

Excellent question — this is a core part of any social media app, and since you’re already building a Flutter + Go + Node.js + Redis + MongoDB architecture, you’re in a great position to implement it cleanly.

Let’s break down how social media platforms (Instagram, Facebook, X, etc.) deliver real-time notifications — and how you can implement it step-by-step in your app.
🧩 1. The Core Concept

There are two layers of notifications:

    Backend event generation → something happens (like, follow, comment, etc.)

    Real-time delivery + storage → user gets notified on app (and optionally via push)

Every “action” in your app (like, comment, follow, challenge, DM) triggers an event that is handled by the notification system.
⚙️ 2. Architecture Overview

Here’s a clean architecture for notifications in your stack:
🔹 Event Source

When any of these happen:

    User A likes User B’s post

    User A follows User B

    User A sends a challenge

    User A comments on User B’s post

→ The service (Go or Node) emits an event message, like:

{
  "type": "like",
  "fromUser": "userA_id",
  "toUser": "userB_id",
  "postId": "post_id",
  "timestamp": 1731325400
}

🔹 Message Queue or Pub/Sub (optional but ideal)

Big apps use Redis Pub/Sub or Kafka/RabbitMQ so notifications can be processed asynchronously.

In your case (since you already have Redis):

    Node.js (or Go) publishes event → notifications_channel

    A Notification Service subscribes to it.

Example:

// Publisher (when like occurs)
redis.Publish(ctx, "notifications", eventJSON)

// Subscriber (notification service)
sub := redis.Subscribe(ctx, "notifications")
for msg := range sub.Channel() {
   handleNotification(msg.Payload)
}

🔹 Notification Storage (MongoDB)

Each user has a notifications collection:

{
  "_id": "notif_123",
  "user_id": "target_user_id",
  "type": "like",
  "from_user": "userA_id",
  "post_id": "post_id",
  "is_read": false,
  "created_at": "2025-11-12T18:30:00Z"
}

This allows you to:

    Display notification list in profile or bell icon.

    Filter unread vs read.

    Mark notifications as read.

🔹 Real-Time Delivery (WebSocket)

Since you’re already using a Node.js WebSocket server, it can:

    Listen for new notifications in Redis (via subscriber)

    Push to the target user instantly if they’re online.

Example flow:

User A likes → Go service publishes to Redis
→ Node WebSocket server subscribed to Redis
→ Sends JSON payload to User B’s socket connection
→ Flutter receives and shows real-time popup

Example WebSocket message:

{
  "event": "notification",
  "data": {
    "type": "like",
    "from_username": "userA",
    "post_id": "xyz"
  }
}

🔹 Push Notifications (Optional but common)

When the user is offline, you can send Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) push notification.

Your Node service can check:

if (!userIsOnline) {
   sendPushNotification(targetUser.fcmToken, "New like from @userA")
}

🔔 3. Flutter Frontend Flow

On Flutter side:

    Connect to WebSocket on login.

    Listen for "notification" events.

    Display a local notification (snackbar, toast, or bell badge).

    Store them locally or fetch via REST /api/notifications.

Example:

channel.stream.listen((message) {
  final data = jsonDecode(message);
  if (data['event'] == 'notification') {
    showNotification(data['data']);
  }
});

🧠 4. Summary of Responsibilities
Layer	Responsibility
Go or Node service	Generate event when action occurs
Redis	Publish/Subscribe for real-time distribution
Notification Service	Store in MongoDB + push via WebSocket
WebSocket Server	Deliver to online users instantly
FCM (optional)	Push to offline users
Flutter App	Listen & display notification
🪄 Example Notification Types
Event Type	Triggered When	Payload Example
like	A likes B’s post	{type:'like', from:'A', postId:'123'}
follow	A follows B	{type:'follow', from:'A'}
comment	A comments on B’s post	{type:'comment', from:'A', postId:'123'}
challenge	A challenges B	{type:'challenge', from:'A', league:'Gold'}

Perfect — that’s an important clarification ✅

So, you’re building your entire backend in Go (Golang) and frontend in Flutter — no Node.js involved. That means you’ll handle notifications, real-time updates, and database operations all inside Go.

Let’s now design the complete Go-based notification system — similar to how big social apps (Instagram, TikTok) do it — but optimized for your current tech stack.
⚙️ OVERVIEW — How Notifications Work in Your Go + Flutter App

Here’s the data flow:

User A likes/follows/comments → Go backend generates event
→ Store in MongoDB (notifications collection)
→ Publish to Redis (for real-time)
→ WebSocket server (Go) pushes to User B if online
→ Flutter client receives live notification

If User B is offline, the next time they open the app, you fetch unread notifications via an API.
🧱 1. Database Design (MongoDB)

You’ll store notifications in a notifications collection.
Each document can look like this:

{
  "_id": "notif_101",
  "user_id": "target_user_id",       // who receives it
  "from_user_id": "actor_user_id",   // who triggered it
  "type": "like",                    // like | follow | comment | challenge
  "post_id": "optional_post_id",
  "is_read": false,
  "created_at": "2025-11-12T18:30:00Z"
}

🧩 2. Redis Pub/Sub (Real-Time Layer)

Since Redis is already part of your architecture, it’s perfect for broadcasting events between your Go microservices.
Example Go Code
Publisher (e.g., inside your Like or Follow handler)

event := map[string]interface{}{
    "type": "like",
    "from_user_id": fromUserID,
    "to_user_id": toUserID,
    "post_id": postID,
    "timestamp": time.Now().Unix(),
}
data, _ := json.Marshal(event)
redisClient.Publish(ctx, "notifications", data)

Subscriber (your WebSocket microservice)

sub := redisClient.Subscribe(ctx, "notifications")
ch := sub.Channel()

for msg := range ch {
    var event map[string]interface{}
    json.Unmarshal([]byte(msg.Payload), &event)

    // Find if the user is connected via WebSocket
    toUserID := event["to_user_id"].(string)
    if conn, ok := activeConnections[toUserID]; ok {
        conn.WriteJSON(event) // send real-time notification
    }

    // Store in MongoDB for persistence
    notificationsCollection.InsertOne(ctx, event)
}

🔌 3. WebSocket Server (Go)

This is the real-time bridge between your backend and Flutter app.
You’ll maintain a map of connected users:

var activeConnections = make(map[string]*websocket.Conn)

Connection Handler

func handleConnections(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    conn, _ := upgrader.Upgrade(w, r, nil)
    userID := r.URL.Query().Get("user_id") // Flutter sends user_id as query param
    activeConnections[userID] = conn

    defer func() {
        conn.Close()
        delete(activeConnections, userID)
    }()

    for {
        _, msg, err := conn.ReadMessage()
        if err != nil {
            break
        }
        fmt.Printf("Received message from user %s: %s\n", userID, msg)
    }
}

So now, when a Redis event arrives, you can instantly conn.WriteJSON() to the correct user.
📱 4. Flutter Client Side

In Flutter, you’ll connect using the web_socket_channel package.
Example:

import 'package:web_socket_channel/io.dart';
import 'dart:convert';

late IOWebSocketChannel channel;

void connectWebSocket(String userId) {
  channel = IOWebSocketChannel.connect(
    Uri.parse('ws://your-server-ip:8080/ws?user_id=$userId'),
  );

  channel.stream.listen((message) {
    final data = jsonDecode(message);
    if (data['type'] == 'like') {
      // Show local notification or snackbar
      print("New Like from ${data['from_user_id']}");
    }
  });
}

This allows instant notification delivery while the app is open.
📡 5. REST API for Notifications (for offline users)

When the user opens the app, Flutter should call an endpoint like:

GET /api/notifications?user_id=123

Go Handler

func GetNotifications(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    userID := r.URL.Query().Get("user_id")
    ctx := context.TODO()

    cur, err := notificationsCollection.Find(ctx, bson.M{"user_id": userID})
    if err != nil {
        http.Error(w, err.Error(), http.StatusInternalServerError)
        return
    }

    var notifs []bson.M
    cur.All(ctx, &notifs)
    json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(notifs)
}

🔔 6. Summary of Full Flow
Step	Action	Component
1	User likes/follows/comments	Go API
2	Publish event to Redis	Go API
3	Redis notifies WebSocket service	Go WebSocket
4	WebSocket pushes to online users	Go WebSocket + Flutter
5	MongoDB stores for persistence	Notification microservice
6	Flutter fetches offline notifications via API	REST endpoint
🚀 7. Optional Enhancements

    Unread counter → store unread count in Redis for quick display

    Mark as read API → /api/notifications/read

    Push notifications → integrate Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) later for offline alerts

    Notification batching → group similar events (“5 people liked your post”)

That’ll give you a working baseline you can plug directly into your backend.

so you are saying whenever someone done any action it stores details in database what it does and when other user get online by checking if websocket is connected then it send those notifications to target user

✅ Exactly right — you’ve got it perfectly.

Let’s walk through that logic clearly, step by step — this is exactly how modern social media platforms do it internally (Instagram, Twitter/X, TikTok, etc.), and it fits your Go + Flutter stack beautifully.
🧠 Concept in Simple Terms

Think of notifications as “records of actions” stored in your backend.

Whenever any user does something meaningful (like, follow, comment, challenge), your backend creates a record of that event — that’s the notification.

Then, you have two situations:
Situation	What Happens
Target user is online	WebSocket connection is active → send it instantly (real-time)
Target user is offline	Just store it in DB → send later when they come online or fetch via API
🔄 Step-by-Step Flow
🧩 1. An action happens

Example:
User A likes User B’s post.

Your Go API that handles likes will:

    Save the “like” itself (post_likes table)

    Create a notification record in MongoDB (or PostgreSQL)

    Publish a Redis message for real-time delivery.

notification := Notification{
    UserID:     targetUserID,    // who will receive
    FromUserID: actorUserID,     // who triggered
    Type:       "like",
    PostID:     postID,
    IsRead:     false,
    CreatedAt:  time.Now(),
}

// 1️⃣ Store in DB
notificationsCollection.InsertOne(ctx, notification)

// 2️⃣ Publish event
eventJSON, _ := json.Marshal(notification)
redisClient.Publish(ctx, "notifications", eventJSON)

🔌 2. WebSocket server is subscribed to Redis

Your Go WebSocket service listens to Redis:

sub := redisClient.Subscribe(ctx, "notifications")
ch := sub.Channel()

for msg := range ch {
    var notif Notification
    json.Unmarshal([]byte(msg.Payload), &notif)

    // check if user is connected
    if conn, ok := activeConnections[notif.UserID]; ok {
        conn.WriteJSON(notif)  // 🔥 instant delivery
    }
}

🧭 3. Online user receives instantly

On the Flutter side, if User B is currently using the app and connected to WebSocket:

channel.stream.listen((message) {
  final data = jsonDecode(message);
  print("🔥 New notification: ${data['type']} from ${data['from_user_id']}");
});

User B sees:

    “@UserA liked your post”

Instantly — no refresh, no polling.
💤 4. Offline user gets it later

If User B is not connected, the WebSocket check (activeConnections[userID]) fails.

But that’s fine — because you already stored the notification in MongoDB.

So when they open the app later, Flutter just calls:

GET /api/notifications?user_id=UserB

and the backend returns all unread notifications.
📱 5. User opens app → WebSocket reconnects

When User B opens the app, the Flutter app:

    Connects WebSocket (/ws?user_id=B)

    Fetches unread notifications (/api/notifications?user_id=B)

    Marks them as read if user opens them (PATCH /api/notifications/read)

🔐 6. Summary — What You Understood (and It’s 100% Correct)

✅ When someone performs an action → backend logs it (DB + Redis).
✅ If the target user is online (WebSocket active) → instantly push it.
✅ If offline → just keep it in DB → send next time via REST fetch or on reconnect.

This ensures no notification is lost, and users always get consistent behavior.

/notifications/
   model.go
   handler.go
   websocket.go
   redis.go

so you can just integrate it directly into your Go backend?

Explain it in a way like what happens when we click follow or like button in frontend how it send trigger event to backend and in backend how it handles and stores all those triggers and in what way how they send it to target user all that

Perfect — you want a real-world walkthrough of what exactly happens when a user taps “Follow” or “Like” in a social media app — from the moment the user clicks the button in the frontend, all the way to when the target user gets a notification.

Let’s go through it end-to-end, step by step, in your Go + Flutter architecture.
🚀 FULL FLOW: “User A follows User B”

Let’s say:
👉 User A (actor) clicks “Follow” on User B (target)

We’ll break this down into Frontend → API → Backend → Notification → Real-time delivery
🧩 1. FRONTEND (Flutter)
🖱 User Action

User A taps the Follow button in Flutter:

ElevatedButton(
  onPressed: () async {
    final response = await http.post(
      Uri.parse("http://yourserver.com/api/follow"),
      body: {
        "follower_id": currentUserId,
        "following_id": targetUserId
      },
    );
  },
  child: Text("Follow"),
)

So, Flutter simply sends a POST request to your Go backend with two IDs:

    follower_id: the one who clicked Follow (User A)

    following_id: the one being followed (User B)

⚙️ 2. BACKEND (Go API)

When /api/follow endpoint receives this POST:
Step 1️⃣ — Save the relationship in DB

In your PostgreSQL or MongoDB:

// Example structure
type Follow struct {
    FollowerID string `bson:"follower_id"`
    FollowingID string `bson:"following_id"`
    CreatedAt time.Time `bson:"created_at"`
}

follow := Follow{
    FollowerID: followerID,
    FollowingID: followingID,
    CreatedAt: time.Now(),
}
followsCollection.InsertOne(ctx, follow)

So now, the “Follow” relationship exists in your database.
Step 2️⃣ — Create a Notification Object

Immediately after saving, your backend creates a notification record for the target user (User B):

notif := Notification{
    UserID:     followingID,   // target (B)
    FromUserID: followerID,    // actor (A)
    Type:       "follow",
    IsRead:     false,
    CreatedAt:  time.Now(),
}
notificationsCollection.InsertOne(ctx, notif)

So now MongoDB has:

{
  "user_id": "UserB",
  "from_user_id": "UserA",
  "type": "follow",
  "is_read": false,
  "created_at": "2025-11-12T22:00:00Z"
}

Step 3️⃣ — Publish an Event to Redis

This lets the notification system or WebSocket service know something happened.

event, _ := json.Marshal(notif)
redisClient.Publish(ctx, "notifications", event)

That means you’re saying:

    “Hey, User A followed User B — whoever handles notifications, take care of it.”

⚙️ 3. BACKEND (Notification Handler / WebSocket Service)

You have a Go WebSocket service subscribed to Redis.
It’s always listening for new messages in the "notifications" channel.
Example:

sub := redisClient.Subscribe(ctx, "notifications")
ch := sub.Channel()

for msg := range ch {
    var notif Notification
    json.Unmarshal([]byte(msg.Payload), &notif)

    // Check if the target user (User B) is online
    if conn, ok := activeConnections[notif.UserID]; ok {
        // send instantly through websocket
        conn.WriteJSON(notif)
    } else {
        // if offline, do nothing (already saved in DB)
    }
}

⚡ 4. REAL-TIME DELIVERY (WebSocket)

If User B is currently using the app and has a WebSocket connection open, then your WebSocket server sends the notification instantly:

{
  "type": "follow",
  "from_user_id": "UserA",
  "message": "UserA started following you."
}

On the Flutter side, you already have a listener like:

channel.stream.listen((message) {
  final data = jsonDecode(message);
  if (data['type'] == 'follow') {
    showNotification("New follower!", "${data['from_user_id']} followed you");
  }
});

So User B instantly sees a popup or badge — real-time!
💤 5. IF USER IS OFFLINE

If User B is not connected to WebSocket, the backend doesn’t find their connection in activeConnections.

That’s fine — because:

    The notification is already saved in MongoDB

    When User B next opens the app, Flutter calls:

    GET /api/notifications?user_id=UserB

    The backend fetches all notifications:

    cur, _ := notificationsCollection.Find(ctx, bson.M{"user_id": userID})
    cur.All(ctx, &results)
    json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(results)

    Flutter shows them in the Notification screen.

❤️ LIKE EVENT — Similar Logic

When User A likes User B’s post:
Frontend

await http.post(
  Uri.parse("http://yourserver.com/api/like"),
  body: {
    "user_id": currentUserId,
    "post_id": postId
  },
);

Backend (Go)

    Save like record in post_likes

    Get the post’s owner (targetUserID)

    Create notification (type: "like")

    Publish to Redis (notifications channel)

    WebSocket server pushes it instantly if target is online

🔄 SUMMARY — Entire Chain of Events
Step	Component	What Happens
1	Flutter UI	User taps “Follow” or “Like”
2	Go API	Receives request, validates user
3	Go API	Saves action (follow/like) in DB
4	Go API	Creates notification record (MongoDB)
5	Go API	Publishes event to Redis
6	Go WebSocket	Subscribed to Redis, receives event
7	WebSocket Server	Sends to target user instantly if online
8	MongoDB	Persists all notifications
9	Flutter App	Listens to WebSocket or fetches via REST
10	User B sees notification	Real-time or next time they open app
📬 Real-world Analogy

It’s like a postal + live chat combo system:

    You (User A) send a “Follow letter”.

    The backend (post office) logs it and stores it in the target’s mailbox (DB).

    If the target (User B) is online (door open via WebSocket), the postman delivers immediately.

    If they’re offline, the letter just stays in their mailbox.

    When they open the app, they “check their mailbox” (GET /notifications).

Would you like me to show this flow as a diagram (visual) — arrows from Flutter → Go API → Redis → WebSocket → Flutter — so it’s easier to visualize the data movement?

So when multiple user send notification to simultaneously what happens 

Excellent — this is the right next question 👏

You’re now thinking about concurrency and scalability, which are exactly what make social apps reliable when thousands (or millions) of users generate notifications at the same time.

Let’s go step-by-step through what happens when many users trigger notifications simultaneously (e.g. 10 people follow or like someone at once) — and how your Go + Redis + MongoDB setup handles that safely and efficiently.
⚙️ 1. Multiple Users Trigger Actions at Once

Let’s say 10 different users (UserA1 → UserA10) all like posts belonging to UserB within the same second.

So, from the frontend:

    10 separate HTTP POST requests hit your /api/like endpoint almost simultaneously.

In Go, each request runs in its own goroutine — Go’s built-in concurrency system.

func LikePostHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    go processLike(r) // handled concurrently
}

Each goroutine does:

    Saves the like in DB

    Creates a notification record

    Publishes an event to Redis

Go’s goroutines are lightweight — they can handle thousands of concurrent events easily.
🧩 2. Redis Pub/Sub Handles Many Messages in Parallel

Each of those goroutines calls:

redisClient.Publish(ctx, "notifications", eventJSON)

Redis queues all these messages and delivers them (in order) to every subscriber — like your WebSocket service.

Redis is extremely fast (handles millions of messages per second), so even if 10,000 notifications arrive in one second, they’re processed sequentially but instantly.
⚡ 3. WebSocket Service (Subscriber) Receives a Stream of Events

Your WebSocket service is subscribed to Redis and has a single listener loop:

sub := redisClient.Subscribe(ctx, "notifications")
ch := sub.Channel()

for msg := range ch {
    go handleNotification(msg.Payload) // each message handled in its own goroutine
}

Each handleNotification() runs in its own goroutine — again, fully concurrent — so 10+ messages can be processed simultaneously.

This function:

    Parses the message

    Saves it in MongoDB (if not already stored)

    Checks if the user is online (activeConnections)

    If yes → sends via conn.WriteJSON()

💾 4. MongoDB Stores Them Separately

Each incoming event is inserted as a new document in the notifications collection.

Example (10 notifications for UserB):

[
  {"from_user": "A1", "to_user": "B", "type": "like"},
  {"from_user": "A2", "to_user": "B", "type": "like"},
  ...
  {"from_user": "A10", "to_user": "B", "type": "like"}
]

MongoDB can handle multiple writes in parallel — especially if you’re using one shared connection pool.
Each write is atomic, so no two notifications overwrite each other.
🔌 5. WebSocket Pushes Them Out — Instantly and Safely

If UserB is online and has a WebSocket connection open:

if conn, ok := activeConnections[userID]; ok {
    conn.WriteJSON(event)
}

Multiple goroutines might call WriteJSON to the same connection nearly at the same time — so you usually wrap this in a mutex lock to avoid race conditions:

var connMutex sync.Mutex

connMutex.Lock()
conn.WriteJSON(event)
connMutex.Unlock()

That ensures each notification is sent one after another safely.
📨 6. Flutter App Receives Notifications in Sequence

The Flutter app’s WebSocket listener gets multiple messages back-to-back:

channel.stream.listen((message) {
  final data = jsonDecode(message);
  print("New notification: ${data['from_user_id']} ${data['type']}");
});

If 10 likes happen, it receives 10 separate messages — near real-time (milliseconds apart).

You can choose to:

    Show each individually (“@userA liked your post”)

    Or group them later (“10 people liked your post”)

🧠 7. Key Takeaways (Concurrency Safety)
Step	How it Handles Simultaneous Events
Go API	Goroutines allow thousands of concurrent actions
Redis Pub/Sub	Buffers + broadcasts all messages quickly
WebSocket Service	Handles messages concurrently (via goroutines)
MongoDB	Each insert is atomic and concurrent-safe
WebSocket Delivery	Mutex around connection write avoids collision
Flutter App	Processes incoming events as a stream (sequentially)
🔒 8. Optional Scaling Techniques (for Future Growth)

If your app grows big:

    Use a Redis Stream (instead of Pub/Sub) → guaranteed delivery, replayable messages

    Add a notification microservice that only handles notification creation

    Use message batching (“5 new likes”) to reduce spam

    Add a rate limiter to prevent abuse

    Use load balancer + multiple WebSocket servers (with Redis for shared state)

In short:

    Even when 1,000 people like or follow someone at once, Go handles each action in parallel, Redis queues the events, MongoDB saves them individually, and WebSocket delivers them instantly or later when the user is online — all safely and efficiently.

Would you like me to show you a visual flow diagram of this “multi-user simultaneous notification” process (from frontend clicks → concurrent Go handlers → Redis → WebSocket → Flutter)? It’ll make this whole flow super clear.

So what is websocket or what is http api call how it connects. Does it with websocket or rest api

Excellent question 👏 — this is a key concept in how social media apps handle real-time notifications and user interactions.
Let’s break it down step-by-step, especially in your case (Flutter frontend + Go backend).
🧩 1. What’s the difference between HTTP API and WebSocket?
Concept	HTTP API (REST)	WebSocket
Connection Type	One request → one response. Then connection closes.	Persistent, continuous two-way connection.
Direction	Client → Server only (server replies once).	Both Client ↔ Server (real-time).
Use Case	Signup, login, like button, follow button, fetching feed, etc.	Notifications, messages, live updates, typing indicators, etc.
Analogy	Like making a phone call, saying one sentence, and hanging up.	Like staying on a call continuously to talk both ways.
⚙️ 2. What happens when you click “Follow” or “Like” (with Go + Flutter)

Let’s go step-by-step:
Step 1 – Flutter sends REST API call

When user clicks the Follow button:

await http.post(
  Uri.parse("https://api.yourapp.com/follow"),
  body: {
    "follower_id": currentUserId,
    "following_id": targetUserId,
  },
);

So Flutter → sends a POST request to Go backend (via HTTP API).
Step 2 – Go Backend handles the request

Your Go server has a REST endpoint, for example:

func FollowHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    // Parse data
    followerID := r.FormValue("follower_id")
    followingID := r.FormValue("following_id")

    // Store in database
    _, err := db.Exec(`INSERT INTO follows (follower_id, following_id) VALUES ($1, $2)`, followerID, followingID)
    if err != nil {
        http.Error(w, err.Error(), 500)
        return
    }

    // Create a notification
    _, err = db.Exec(`INSERT INTO notifications (user_id, type, from_user, created_at) VALUES ($1, $2, $3, NOW())`,
        followingID, "follow", followerID)
}

✅ It does two things:

    Stores the follow in the follows table.

    Creates a notification entry in notifications table.

Step 3 – Notify target user (via WebSocket)

If the target user (the one being followed) is online — i.e., they have a WebSocket connection open — then you can instantly send them a real-time notification.

You’ll maintain a map of online users in your Go WebSocket server:

var clients = make(map[string]*websocket.Conn)

When you create the notification:

if conn, ok := clients[followingID]; ok {
    // user is online
    message := map[string]string{
        "type": "notification",
        "event": "follow",
        "from_user": followerID,
    }
    conn.WriteJSON(message)
}

✅ This pushes a live notification to the Flutter app instantly.
Step 4 – Flutter receives real-time message

In Flutter, your WebSocket listener will receive:

channel.stream.listen((message) {
  var data = jsonDecode(message);
  if (data['type'] == 'notification') {
    // show snackbar, update bell icon count, etc.
  }
});

Now the user instantly sees:

    “@alex followed you”

without reloading or refreshing anything.
⚡ When multiple users send notifications simultaneously

Each notification is:

    Stored separately in the notifications table.

    Sent to the user’s WebSocket connection (if active).

    If not online → stays in DB → delivered when user reconnects.

So if 100 users like your photo at once:

    100 API calls are made to the backend.

    Each creates 1 DB entry.

    Each triggers conn.WriteJSON() to you (if you’re online).

    If you’re offline, you’ll see all 100 when you open the app next (via /api/get-notifications).

🧠 Summary
Step	Technology	Role
1️⃣	REST API (HTTP)	Send user action to backend (like/follow/comment)
2️⃣	Database	Save action + create notification record
3️⃣	WebSocket	Instantly deliver live update to target user
4️⃣	REST API (backup)	Fetch missed notifications when offline

If you want, I can also show you a Go WebSocket + REST API combined example, where you can plug it directly into your backend to handle both live and stored notifications.
