So why don't we use websocket if it constantly created connection rather than rest api. How does it connect with database does it do with rest api or websocket or is there any other method. And tell me if there is any other method for communication like restapi or websocket or etc.

Excellent — that’s a very deep backend engineering question 👍
Let’s go step-by-step and really clear this up properly.
🧠 Why we don’t use only WebSockets for everything

At first glance, it seems logical:

    “If WebSocket can send and receive continuously, why not use it for all communication?”

Here’s why we don’t 👇
Reason	Explanation
1. WebSocket is persistent	Every open WebSocket keeps a connection alive between client ↔ server. If you have 1 million users, that’s 1 million open sockets consuming memory and CPU. We don’t need that for signup/login/fetch feed — those are one-time operations.
2. REST API is simpler and cheaper for one-shot actions	Things like “login”, “create post”, “get profile” happen rarely and don’t need a continuous stream. A single HTTP request is lighter.
3. Security & scalability	Load balancers, caches (Cloudflare, Nginx, etc.), and proxies handle HTTP easily; WebSockets need special routing and are harder to scale.
4. Reliability	If the user loses connection, the WebSocket must reconnect, re-authenticate, and resync — more complexity. REST is stateless and simpler.

So the usual pattern:

    ✅ REST API → for regular actions (CRUD, authentication, uploading, etc.)

    ⚡ WebSocket → for real-time updates (notifications, chat, live likes)

    🗃️ Database → stores all state changes permanently

🔄 How database fits into this

Both REST and WebSocket routes talk to the same backend logic and database — the difference is how data is delivered.
REST API → Database flow

Flutter → HTTP POST → Go API → SQL (insert/update/select)

Example:

func CreatePost(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    db.Exec("INSERT INTO posts (user_id, caption) VALUES ($1, $2)", userID, caption)
}

WebSocket → Database flow

If the data comes via a WebSocket message (for example, in-app chat):

Flutter → WS message → Go WebSocket server → SQL (insert) + broadcast to others

Example:

func handleMessage(conn *websocket.Conn, message []byte) {
    var msg ChatMessage
    json.Unmarshal(message, &msg)
    db.Exec("INSERT INTO messages (sender_id, receiver_id, text) VALUES ($1,$2,$3)",
        msg.SenderID, msg.ReceiverID, msg.Text)

    // Then broadcast via WebSocket
    if targetConn, ok := clients[msg.ReceiverID]; ok {
        targetConn.WriteJSON(msg)
    }
}

So, both REST and WebSocket ultimately connect to the same database — just via different handlers.
🛰️ Other communication methods used in social apps

There are a few more you should know about 👇
Method	Direction	Real-time?	Typical Use
HTTP REST API	Client → Server	❌ No	Normal requests (login, fetch feed, post upload)
WebSocket	Client ↔ Server	✅ Yes	Chat, notifications, live events
gRPC (over HTTP/2)	Client ↔ Server	✅ Yes	High-performance internal service communication (microservices)
Server-Sent Events (SSE)	Server → Client (one-way)	✅ Yes	Real-time data stream (e.g., live feed updates)
MQTT	Client ↔ Broker	✅ Yes	Lightweight IoT or messaging apps (Telegram, WhatsApp use it)
GraphQL (over HTTP or WS)	Client ↔ Server	Optional	Flexible API queries
WebRTC	Peer ↔ Peer	✅ Yes	Voice/video calls, live streams
Push Notifications (FCM/APNs)	Server → Device OS	✅ Yes (but external)	Notify offline users via phone OS
⚙️ In your case (Go + Flutter)

Here’s how you’ll likely combine them:
Action	Communication	Description
Signup/Login	REST API	One-time HTTP call to authenticate user.
Follow / Like / Comment	REST API	Store in DB + trigger WebSocket notification to receiver.
Notifications / Chat	WebSocket	Live delivery to online users.
Offline users	REST API	Fetch notifications missed while offline.
Future (optional)	FCM Push	Send “You got a new follower” even when app closed.
🔑 In summary

    REST API → stateless, short, simple.

    WebSocket → continuous, bi-directional, real-time.

    Database → the single source of truth.

    Push / Queues / Events → make it scalable when many actions happen.

If you want, I can show you an architecture diagram that visually connects all this — from Flutter button click → Go REST → DB → WebSocket push → Flutter notification.
Would you like me to draw that out?

Where fcm push is used 

Perfect question 🔥 — because FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging) is the piece that connects your backend to the user’s device when the app isn’t running or the user is offline.

Let’s go step-by-step and tie it to your Go + Flutter social app 👇
📱 What is FCM?

Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) is a push notification service by Google.
It lets your backend server send notifications directly to users’ phones — even if:

    the app is closed,

    the phone is locked, or

    the user has no active WebSocket connection.

⚙️ How it fits into your social app flow

Let’s see the full lifecycle — from the moment someone performs an action.
🔹 1. User performs an action (e.g., “follow”)

Frontend (Flutter):

await http.post(
  Uri.parse("https://api.yourapp.com/follow"),
  body: {
    "follower_id": currentUserId,
    "following_id": targetUserId,
  },
);

🔹 2. Go backend handles it

Your Go backend receives the request:

func FollowHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    followerID := r.FormValue("follower_id")
    followingID := r.FormValue("following_id")

    // Save follow in DB
    db.Exec(`INSERT INTO follows (follower_id, following_id) VALUES ($1,$2)`, followerID, followingID)

    // Create notification in DB
    db.Exec(`INSERT INTO notifications (user_id, type, from_user, created_at) VALUES ($1,$2,$3,NOW())`,
        followingID, "follow", followerID)

    // Trigger FCM Push (for offline users)
    go sendFCMNotification(followingID, fmt.Sprintf("%s started following you", followerID))
}

🔹 3. Backend calls FCM

In your Go backend, you’ll use the Firebase Admin SDK or an HTTP POST to the FCM API:

func sendFCMNotification(targetUserID, message string) {
    token := getFCMTokenFromDB(targetUserID) // stored when user logged in

    fcmMessage := map[string]interface{}{
        "to": token,
        "notification": map[string]string{
            "title": "New Follower",
            "body":  message,
        },
    }

    // Send POST to FCM server
    http.Post("https://fcm.googleapis.com/fcm/send", "application/json", bytes.NewBuffer(jsonData))
}

✅ This will cause Google’s FCM servers to deliver the message to the user’s phone.
🔹 4. Flutter receives push notification (via OS)

Even if the app is closed, Android/iOS shows a push banner:

    🔔 “@alex started following you”

When the user taps it → Flutter app opens → you fetch all notifications via REST or WebSocket.
🧩 When FCM is used vs when WebSocket is used
Case	WebSocket	FCM
App is open & connected	✅ Real-time message via WebSocket	❌ Not used
App is backgrounded (running but not visible)	⚠️ Sometimes active	✅ Used to wake app / show banner
App is closed or phone locked	❌ WebSocket disconnected	✅ Used
Internet is off	❌ Can’t deliver	⚠️ FCM retries later
🧠 Where FCM fits in your architecture

[Flutter app]
     ↑
  WebSocket (real-time when open)
     ↑
[Go backend] —> Database
     │
     └──> FCM Push (via Google servers)
               ↓
           [User’s phone OS]

So FCM isn’t an alternative to WebSocket or REST —
👉 it’s a backup channel for offline or background notification delivery.
🗃️ How you manage it

Every user’s fcm_token is stored in your DB:

ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN fcm_token TEXT;

When they log in, Flutter registers it:

String? token = await FirebaseMessaging.instance.getToken();
await http.post(
  Uri.parse("https://api.yourapp.com/update-token"),
  body: {"user_id": userId, "fcm_token": token},
);

Then your Go backend can look it up anytime to push notifications.
✅ Summary
Component	Role
REST API	For one-time actions like follow/like/comment
WebSocket	Real-time updates when users are online
FCM Push	Notify offline users via Android/iOS
Database	Stores all notifications permanently

If you want, I can show you how to integrate FCM push with your Go backend (with Firebase Admin SDK setup and example code).
Would you like that next?

What fcm token represent and how user get notification when he is offline 

Perfect question again 💡 — this shows you’re thinking like a backend architect now.
Let’s break this down in simple but complete terms — so you fully understand what an FCM token is, how it works, and how offline notifications reach users.
🔑 What is an FCM token?

Think of the FCM token as your app’s unique address on the Firebase Cloud Messaging network.

    Every installation of your Flutter app on a phone has its own unique FCM token.

    It’s like a temporary ID that Google/Firebase assigns to that specific device + app instance.

    When you (the backend) want to send a notification to a user, you don’t talk to the phone directly — you tell Firebase:

        “Hey Firebase, deliver this message to the device with this token.”

Then Firebase finds that device and delivers the push.
📱 Analogy: Think of FCM tokens like WhatsApp phone numbers
Concept	Analogy
FCM token	Your WhatsApp phone number
FCM server	WhatsApp’s central server
Backend (your Go app)	Someone sending you a message
Device	Your phone

So if your backend says:

    Send a message to token f02a93b5:APA91b...

Firebase knows exactly which device and which app instance to deliver that notification to.
⚙️ How the FCM token is created (in Flutter)

When your app first starts, the Firebase SDK automatically generates it:

import 'package:firebase_messaging/firebase_messaging.dart';

void initFCM() async {
  String? token = await FirebaseMessaging.instance.getToken();
  print("User FCM Token: $token");
}

Then you send it to your backend:

await http.post(
  Uri.parse("https://api.yourapp.com/update-fcm-token"),
  body: {
    "user_id": userId,
    "fcm_token": token,
  },
);

🗄️ How you store it in your backend (Go + PostgreSQL)

You add a column:

ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN fcm_token TEXT;

When a user logs in:

func UpdateFCMToken(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
    userID := r.FormValue("user_id")
    token := r.FormValue("fcm_token")

    _, err := db.Exec(`UPDATE users SET fcm_token=$1 WHERE id=$2`, token, userID)
    if err != nil {
        http.Error(w, err.Error(), 500)
        return
    }

    fmt.Fprint(w, "Token updated successfully")
}

Now your backend knows exactly which device belongs to which user.
🌐 What happens when the user is offline

Let’s walk through it clearly 👇
🧩 Step 1 – Backend triggers FCM

Someone performs an action — say Alex follows John.

Your backend inserts the “follow” in the database, and then looks up John’s FCM token:

token := getUserFCMToken("john_id")
sendFCMNotification(token, "Alex started following you!")

🧩 Step 2 – Your Go backend sends the notification to Firebase

fcmMessage := map[string]interface{}{
    "to": token,
    "notification": map[string]string{
        "title": "New Follower",
        "body":  "Alex started following you!",
    },
}

Firebase receives this message and queues it in its system.
🧩 Step 3 – Firebase delivers it to the OS (Android/iOS)

Even though John’s app is closed or offline, the Firebase background service (built into Android or iOS) is always running in the OS level.

Firebase keeps a secure connection with the device and holds the notification until:

    the phone reconnects to the internet,

    and the device’s FCM service receives the message.

Then it shows it as a push banner (like WhatsApp or Instagram do).
🧩 Step 4 – User taps the notification

When the user taps it:

    The app opens (even if it was closed)

    You can fetch new notifications via your /api/get-notifications endpoint

    You can clear the badge or show an in-app alert

🧠 Summary
Concept	Description
FCM Token	Unique ID for your app instance on that device.
Who issues it	Firebase SDK automatically.
Where it’s stored	Your database (users.fcm_token).
When used	When backend sends push via Firebase to that user.
Offline behavior	Firebase holds message → delivers when user reconnects.
When refreshed	Token may change (reinstall, clear data, logout) — so update backend.
⚡ Typical flow in your social app

(1) User installs app → gets FCM token
(2) Flutter → sends token to Go backend
(3) Go backend stores it in DB
(4) When another user likes/follows/comments
      → backend sends push via FCM
(5) Firebase delivers to user’s phone (even if app closed)

🔐 One last key thing

    FCM tokens can expire or rotate. So your Flutter app should refresh token periodically:

FirebaseMessaging.instance.onTokenRefresh.listen((newToken) {
  updateTokenToBackend(newToken);
});

Would you like me to show you a complete Go function that sends an FCM push to a user (using Firebase Admin SDK), so you can plug it into your backend directly?

What else i can use for push notification like apn 

Fantastic follow-up 💡 — yes, FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging) isn’t the only way to deliver push notifications. It’s just the most convenient cross-platform one.

Let’s break down what other options exist — and when you’d use each — especially for a Go + Flutter social app like yours.
🚀 1. FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging)

✅ You already know this one.

    Platform: Android, iOS, Web

    Who owns it: Google

    How it works: You send a message → Google’s FCM servers → user’s device.

    Pros:

        Free

        Works on both Android & iOS (through Apple APNs internally)

        Easy Flutter SDK

    Cons:

        Requires Google services (not ideal for China)

        Slight latency in delivery at times

🍎 2. APNs (Apple Push Notification service)

    Platform: iOS, macOS, watchOS

    Who owns it: Apple

    How it works:
    Your server connects directly to Apple’s APNs endpoint via HTTP/2 with a JWT or certificate, and Apple delivers it to iPhones.

    Pros:

        Official Apple service → best reliability on iOS

        Fine-grained control (mutable content, rich notifications, etc.)

    Cons:

        iOS only

        Requires Apple developer credentials

        More setup (certificates or tokens)

    Note: FCM on iOS actually uses APNs behind the scenes.
    When you send a message through FCM, Google routes it to APNs for delivery.

So if you already use FCM → you’re indirectly using APNs too for iPhones.
☁️ 3. OneSignal

    Type: Third-party push notification service

    Platforms: Android, iOS, Web, Flutter

    How it works: You integrate OneSignal SDK → OneSignal uses FCM/APNs internally.

    Pros:

        Simple integration

        Analytics, targeting, segments, A/B testing

    Cons:

        Vendor-lock (you rely on their dashboard)

        Paid for large volumes

Good for small to mid-size apps where you want dashboards & analytics easily.
📨 4. Amazon SNS (Simple Notification Service)

    Platform: Cross-platform (uses FCM, APNs, ADM, etc.)

    How it works: Unified interface for multiple push providers.

    Pros:

        Enterprise-grade, scalable

        Works with FCM, APNs, Amazon Device Messaging, and email/SMS

    Cons:

        More setup and cost

        Complex IAM permissions

🧩 5. Web Push / VAPID

    Platform: Web browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari)

    Use case: Progressive Web Apps (PWA)

    Pros: Doesn’t need mobile SDKs

    Cons: Only for browsers, not native Flutter apps

💬 6. Self-hosted brokers (for advanced setups)

If you want to run everything yourself, you can use:

    MQTT brokers (like Mosquitto, EMQX, or HiveMQ)
    → Used in WhatsApp-like architectures.

    Web Push Gateways (self-built over WebSockets)
    → Real-time in-app messaging, not OS-level notifications.

But:

    MQTT/WebSocket notifications only work while app is open.
    They can’t wake a closed app like FCM/APNs.

🧠 Summary table
Service	Platform	Real-time (when app open)	Offline Delivery	Best For
FCM	Android + iOS + Web	✅	✅	General use (your app)
APNs	iOS only	✅	✅	Native iOS apps
OneSignal	All	✅	✅	Easy setup, analytics
Amazon SNS	All	✅	✅	Enterprise apps
Web Push	Browser only	✅	✅	PWAs / websites
MQTT / WebSocket	All	✅	❌	In-app live messages only
⚙️ For your Go + Flutter app

You can choose between two main options:
Option A – FCM only (Recommended)

    Flutter SDK ready.

    Works for both Android and iOS.

    Your Go backend just sends one type of push.

Option B – Dual stack (for more control)

    Send directly to APNs for iOS users.

    Send to FCM for Android users.

    Your database stores which platform each user uses:

    users(id, platform, fcm_token, apns_token)

    Then your Go backend chooses the right service per user.

If you want, I can show you a Go example of sending push notifications via APNs directly (without FCM) so you can see how that works under the hood.
Would you like me to show that next?

package main

import (
	"encoding/json"
	"net/http"
	"sort"
	"strings"
)

// scoredUser is a helper struct just for sorting users with their search score.
// It is not exported and only used within this file.
type scoredUser struct {
	User  User
	Score float64
}

// SearchHandler handles requests to the /search endpoint.
func SearchHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
	query := r.URL.Query().Get("q")

	if query == "" {
		http.Error(w, "Missing search query parameter 'q'", http.StatusBadRequest)
		return
	}

	var scoredUsers []scoredUser

	// Use the global 'users' slice from database.go
	for _, user := range users {
		score := calculateScore(user, query)
		if score > 0 {
			scoredUsers = append(scoredUsers, scoredUser{User: user, Score: score})
		}
	}

	// Sort users based on their score in descending order.
	sort.Slice(scoredUsers, func(i, j int) bool {
		return scoredUsers[i].Score > scoredUsers[j].Score
	})

	// Extract the User objects from the sorted list for the response.
	resultUsers := make([]User, len(scoredUsers))
	for i, su := range scoredUsers {
		resultUsers[i] = su.User
	}

	// The SearchResponse struct is defined in models.go
	response := SearchResponse{
		Results: resultUsers,
		Total:   len(resultUsers),
	}

	w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
	json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(response)
}

// calculateScore calculates a relevance score for a user based on a search query.
// It now uses the FullName field and no longer considers location.
func calculateScore(user User, query string) float64 {
	var score float64
	query = strings.ToLower(query)

	// Strong match for username
	if strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(user.Username), query) {
		score += 10.0
	}
	
	// Weaker match for full name
	if strings.Contains(strings.ToLower(user.FullName), query) {
		score += 5.0
	}

	// Add a small bonus for having more followers
	score += float64(user.Followers) * 0.01 // Reduced follower bonus to avoid dominating score

	return score
}
search.go


package main

import (
	"encoding/json"
	"log"
	"net/http"

	"github.com/gorilla/mux"
)

// LoginHandler validates credentials and returns the user's data and all other users.
func LoginHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
	var creds struct {
		Username string json:"username"
		Password string json:"password"
	}

	if err := json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&creds); err != nil {
		http.Error(w, "Invalid request body", http.StatusBadRequest)
		return
	}

	// First, validate the user's credentials.
	if !IsValidUser(creds.Username, creds.Password) {
		http.Error(w, "Invalid username or password", http.StatusUnauthorized)
		log.Printf("Failed login attempt for user %s.", creds.Username)
		return
	}

	// If valid, fetch the logged-in user's full profile.
	user, exists := GetUserByUsername(creds.Username)
	if !exists {
		// This should not happen if IsValidUser passed, but it's good practice to check.
		http.Error(w, "Could not find user data after successful login", http.StatusInternalServerError)
		return
	}

	// Fetch all users for the search/discovery feature.
	allUsers := GetAllUsers()

	// Create the response payload that the Flutter client expects.
	response := map[string]interface{}{
		"user":     user,
		"allUsers": allUsers,
	}

	// Send the successful response.
	w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
	w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)
	json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(response)
	log.Printf("User %s logged in successfully. Sent user profile and all users list.", creds.Username)
}

// GetAllUsersHandler returns a list of all users.
func GetAllUsersHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
	w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
	allUsers := GetAllUsers()
	if err := json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(allUsers); err != nil {
		http.Error(w, "Failed to encode users", http.StatusInternalServerError)
	}
}

// GetUserHandler returns the data for a single user.
func GetUserHandler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
	vars := mux.Vars(r)
	username := vars["username"]

	user, exists := GetUserByUsername(username)
	if !exists {
		http.Error(w, "User not found", http.StatusNotFound)
		return
	}

	w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
	if err := json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(user); err != nil {
		http.Error(w, "Failed to encode user data", http.StatusInternalServerError)
	}
}

// main is the entry point for the application.
func main() {
	InitDatabase()

	r := mux.NewRouter()

	api := r.PathPrefix("/api/v1").Subrouter()
	api.HandleFunc("/users", GetAllUsersHandler).Methods("GET")
	api.HandleFunc("/users/{username}", GetUserHandler).Methods("GET")

	r.HandleFunc("/login", LoginHandler).Methods("POST")
	r.HandleFunc("/ws/{username}", WebsocketHandler).Methods("GET")

	log.Println("Starting server on :8081...")
	if err := http.ListenAndServe(":8081", r); err != nil {
		log.Fatalf("Could not start server: %s\n", err)
	}
}

main.go

Poor Scalability (This is the biggest issue): The algorithm checks every single user in your database for every single search. If you have 100,000 users, it has to perform 100,000 checks every time someone types a letter in the search bar. This will become very slow and expensive as your user base grows.
No Partial Word Matching: It searches for the exact text you type. If you search for "player one", it will not find a user named "The First Player". It doesn't understand that "player" and "one" are separate words.
Basic Relevance: The scoring is quite rigid. It doesn't handle typos or recognize that a shorter username that is a perfect match (e.g., searching "player1" and finding user "player1") is more relevant than a longer username that just contains the query (e.g., finding "anotherplayer123").

what approach should i use to overcome these cons.

Great question — you’ve reached the exact point where real social apps and search systems evolve.
I’ll explain this step-by-step, tied directly to your Go + Flutter backend, and then give you practical approaches you can implement gradually (not theoretical).
First: Why your current search has those problems (short recap)

Your current approach:

for _, user := range users {
    score := calculateScore(user, query)
}

Problems:

    O(N) scan → checks every user → bad at scale

    String contains only → no tokenization, no fuzzy match

    Static scoring → no typo tolerance, no intent understanding

This is fine for learning, but no real app stops here.
How REAL social apps solve this (progressive layers)

Social apps don’t jump directly to Elasticsearch. They evolve in phases.

I’ll give you 4 levels, from simplest → production-grade.
LEVEL 1 — Database-side filtering (Immediate improvement)
Key idea

👉 Move filtering from Go loop → database query

Instead of:

GetAllUsers() → loop in Go

Do:

SELECT * FROM users
WHERE username ILIKE '%query%'
   OR full_name ILIKE '%query%'
LIMIT 50;

Why this helps

    Database uses indexes

    Reduces scanned rows massively

    Faster than Go memory scan

PostgreSQL index (IMPORTANT)

CREATE INDEX idx_users_username ON users USING gin (username gin_trgm_ops);
CREATE INDEX idx_users_fullname ON users USING gin (full_name gin_trgm_ops);

Enable extension:

CREATE EXTENSION pg_trgm;

What this solves

✅ Scalability (big win)
❌ Still weak typo handling
❌ Still basic relevance
LEVEL 2 — Tokenization + weighted scoring (Still no search engine)

Instead of searching full query as one string:
Break query into words

tokens := strings.Fields(strings.ToLower(query))

Example:

"player one" → ["player", "one"]

Score per token

for _, token := range tokens {
    if strings.Contains(username, token) {
        score += 10
    }
    if strings.Contains(fullname, token) {
        score += 5
    }
}

Bonus logic

if username == query {
    score += 50 // exact match
}

Result

    "player one" matches "The First Player"

    Short usernames rank higher

    Still simple, but MUCH better UX

✅ Partial word matching
✅ Better relevance
❌ Still no typo tolerance
LEVEL 3 — Fuzzy matching (Typos) WITHOUT external engines
Use Levenshtein Distance

Example:

func similarity(a, b string) int {
    return levenshtein.Distance(a, b)
}

Then:

distance := levenshtein.Distance(username, query)
if distance <= 2 {
    score += 8
}

What this enables

    "plyaer1" → finds "player1"

    "jon" → finds "john"

⚠️ Use carefully:

    Only for top 50–100 DB results

    NOT on full user table

✅ Typo tolerance
❌ Still not web-scale
LEVEL 4 — Real-world scalable solution (Used by Instagram, Twitter)

At scale, search ≠ database
Architecture

Flutter
  ↓
Search API (Go)
  ↓
Search Engine (Elasticsearch / OpenSearch / Meilisearch)
  ↓
Postgres (source of truth)

Why?

Because search engines provide:

    Inverted indexes

    Fuzzy matching

    Ranking models

    Prefix search

    Fast autocomplete

Recommended search engines (Pick ONE)
🔹 Meilisearch (BEST for you)

    Very easy

    REST API

    Typo-tolerant by default

    Ranking rules configurable

🔹 OpenSearch / Elasticsearch

    Powerful

    More complex

    Heavy ops

How real apps sync data
When user updates profile

1. Save to PostgreSQL
2. Update search index

Example:

func UpdateUser(user User) {
    SaveToDB(user)
    SearchIndex.Upsert(user)
}

Search ranking formula (Production-style)

Final Score =
  Text relevance (username > fullname > bio)
+ Popularity (followers, wins)
+ Interaction signals (search clicks, profile opens)
+ Social proximity (mutual follows)

This is exactly what Instagram does.
Your BEST path forward (Very important)
Step-by-step roadmap for YOU

Now
✅ DB-side search + trigram index
✅ Tokenized scoring
✅ Limit results

Next
✅ Fuzzy match on top results

Later
✅ Meilisearch integration
Example: Final recommended Go search flow

1. Flutter sends query
2. Go API:
   - Query DB with ILIKE + LIMIT 100
   - Tokenize + score
   - Apply fuzzy matching
   - Sort + return top 20

Why this matches your current app

    Works with Go

    Works with Flutter

    No Node.js

    No Redis required

    Scales gradually

    Design search microservice

    Add Meilisearch with Go SDK

    Add search analytics (click tracking)

I want to create a algorithm for suggesting shorts.

Great question 👍
Since you’re building a social app with challenges, leagues, followers, likes, views, comments, and fraud prevention, I’ll design this Shorts (Reels) suggestion algorithm specifically for your architecture and goals, not a generic YouTube copy.
Shorts Suggestion Algorithm (Designed for Your Social App)

Think of this as a scoring + ranking system that runs every time a user opens the Shorts feed or scrolls.
1️⃣ Core Idea (Simple First)

Every short gets a final score for a given user.

Final Score = Engagement Score
            + Personalization Score
            + Social Graph Score
            + Freshness Score
            + Challenge/League Boost
            − Spam/Fraud Penalty

You sort shorts by this score and show the top ones.
2️⃣ Data You Already Have (or Should Have)
Shorts table

shorts (
  id,
  user_id,
  video_url,
  created_at,
  views,
  likes,
  comments,
  shares,
  league,
  challenge_id
)

User interaction table

short_interactions (
  user_id,
  short_id,
  action,       -- view, like, comment, share, skip
  watch_time,   -- seconds watched
  created_at
)

Follows

follows (follower_id, following_id)

3️⃣ Engagement Score (MOST IMPORTANT)

Shorts live or die by watch time.

Engagement Score =
  (watch_time_ratio × 40)
+ (likes × 10)
+ (comments × 15)
+ (shares × 20)
- (skips × 30)

watch_time_ratio

watch_time / video_duration

    A short watched 95% is FAR better than one with many likes but low retention.

4️⃣ Personalization Score (User Taste)

Based on what THIS user likes, not global trends.
Signals

    Shorts user watched fully

    Shorts user liked/commented

    Topics (gaming, dance, tech, comedy)

    Creators user interacts with

Example:

If user likes gaming shorts → +20
If same creator as before → +15
If user skipped similar shorts → -25

This can be stored in Redis:

user_interest:user_id → {gaming: 0.7, comedy: 0.2}

5️⃣ Social Graph Score (Friends & Influence)

People like content from people they know.

If short.creator is followed → +30
If mutual follower → +15
If creator followed by many of user's follows → +10

This makes your app feel alive & social, not random.
6️⃣ Freshness Score (New > Old)

Shorts die fast.

Age < 1 hour      → +40
Age < 6 hours     → +25
Age < 24 hours    → +10
Older than 3 days → -20

This ensures new creators get a chance.
7️⃣ Challenge & League Boost (YOUR UNIQUE EDGE 🔥)

Since your app is gamified, this is powerful.

If short is part of active challenge → +30
If creator league ≥ viewer league → +10
If league battle ongoing → +20

This makes Shorts connected to your core game mechanics, unlike Instagram.
8️⃣ Fraud & Spam Penalty (Very Important)

You already care about fraud 👇

Penalize if:

    Likes from same IP

    Views with 1–2 sec watch time

    Sudden spike with no comments

    Bot-like behavior

If suspicious activity detected → Score × 0.3
If confirmed spam → remove from feed

9️⃣ Final Ranking Flow (Backend)
Step-by-step

    Fetch candidate shorts:

        Recent (last 48h)

        Trending

        Followed creators

    Compute score for each short

    Sort by score

    Paginate (infinite scroll)

    Cache top results in Redis

10️⃣ Simple Go Pseudocode

func CalculateShortScore(short Short, user User) float64 {
    score := 0.0

    score += EngagementScore(short)
    score += PersonalizationScore(short, user)
    score += SocialScore(short, user)
    score += FreshnessScore(short.CreatedAt)
    score += ChallengeBoost(short)
    score -= FraudPenalty(short)

    return score
}

    Freshness

Then add:

    Personalization

    Fraud detection

Perfect follow-up. I’ll explain this in two clear parts, and I’ll map everything back to your current non-ML algorithm so you can upgrade later without rewriting your backend.
PART 1 — How Instagram vs TikTok Shorts Algorithms Differ

They look similar on the surface, but their goals are very different.
🔵 Instagram Reels Algorithm (Social-First)

Instagram is a social graph company.
What Instagram prioritizes

    Who you follow

    Mutual connections

    Creator relationship

    Engagement, but only after social signals

Typical Instagram weighting (simplified)

Social Graph        ~40%
Engagement          ~30%
Personal Interest   ~20%
Freshness           ~10%

What this means

    You see friends & mutuals first

    New creators grow slowly

    Viral reach is limited without social proof

    Works best for people with existing followers

Example

If you follow Creator A, their average Reel can beat a viral Reel from a stranger.
🔴 TikTok Algorithm (Content-First)

TikTok is a recommendation company, not social.
What TikTok prioritizes

    Watch time (completion rate)

    Rewatches

    Instant feedback loops

    User behavior over follower count

Typical TikTok weighting

Watch Time / Retention  ~50%
Interest Similarity     ~30%
Freshness               ~10%
Social Graph             ~5–10%

What this means

    Zero followers can go viral

    One great video beats a big account

    Very aggressive testing of new videos

    Feels addictive because feed is “perfect”

Example

A creator with 0 followers but 95% watch-through → pushed to millions.
🧠 Key Difference Summary
Aspect	Instagram	TikTok
Core focus	Social relationships	Content quality
Followers matter	Yes	Almost no
Virality speed	Slow	Fast
New creators	Hard	Easy
Addiction factor	Medium	Very high
🟣 What YOUR App Should Do (Hybrid Model)

Because you have:

    Followers

    Leagues

    Challenges

    Gamification

Your best model is TikTok content logic + Instagram social logic + your game mechanics.

That’s actually a huge advantage.
PART 2 — Add ML Later WITHOUT Rewriting Everything

This is critical. Most apps fail here.
1️⃣ The Golden Rule (IMPORTANT)

    Never let ML decide the feed directly.

Instead:

    ML outputs scores

    Your existing algorithm combines them

This keeps your system:

    Debuggable

    Controllable

    Cheap

2️⃣ Your Current Algorithm (Rule-Based)

You already have:

Final Score =
  Engagement
+ Personalization
+ Social
+ Freshness
+ League/Challenge
- Fraud

KEEP THIS STRUCTURE FOREVER.
3️⃣ Where ML Fits (Drop-In Modules)

You replace individual score calculators, not the whole pipeline.
Before (rule-based)

func EngagementScore(short Short) float64 {
    return watchTime*40 + likes*10
}

After (ML-powered)

func EngagementScore(short Short, user User) float64 {
    return ml.PredictEngagement(short, user)
}

Same function signature.
No feed rewrite. 🔥
4️⃣ ML Models You Add Gradually
Model 1: Watch-Time Prediction (MOST IMPORTANT)

Predict:

P(user watches > 80%)

Input:

    video length

    past retention

    creator history

    user watch behavior

Output:

0.0 – 1.0

Used as:

EngagementScore = prediction * 100

Model 2: Like / Comment Probability

Predict:

P(user likes | user comments)

Used to rank similar-score videos.
Model 3: Creator Quality Score

Predict:

Creator Consistency Score

Helps:

    Reduce spam

    Promote reliable creators

    Stabilize feed quality

5️⃣ ML Does NOT Replace These ❌

ML should NOT handle:

    League logic

    Challenge rules

    Fraud detection

    Hard limits

    Policy enforcement

These stay rule-based.

Every scroll = training data.

{
  "user_id": "u1",
  "short_id": "s9",
  "watched": true,
  "watch_time": 14,
  "liked": false,
  "skipped": false
}

You can train offline:

    Nightly batch jobs

    Python + PyTorch / TensorFlow

    Store model in S3 / GCS

Flutter App
   ↓

   ↓

Score Returned

ML makes this faster, but rules can do it too.

PART 1 — How to Build the Hybrid Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

Think in layers, not one giant algorithm.
LAYER 1 — Candidate Generation (Who is even eligible?)

Instead of ranking all shorts, you first generate candidates from different sources.

This is how TikTok + Instagram both scale.
Candidate Buckets
Bucket	Purpose	Source
🔥 Trending	Discover viral content	Global stats
👥 Social	Friends & mutuals	Follows table
🎯 Interest	User taste	Past watch history
🆕 Fresh	New creators	Recent uploads
🏆 Game	Challenges & leagues	Your unique edge
Example: Candidate Fetch (Go)

candidates := []Short{}

candidates += FetchTrendingShorts()
candidates += FetchFollowedShorts(userID)
candidates += FetchInterestShorts(userID)
candidates += FetchFreshShorts()
candidates += FetchChallengeShorts(userID)

    This is Instagram-style social filtering + TikTok-style discovery + your game logic.

LAYER 2 — TikTok Content Logic (Content Quality First)

Now evaluate content performance, ignoring followers.
Core TikTok Signals

    Watch time %

    Completion rate

    Rewatches

    Skip rate

    Early engagement velocity

Rule-Based Score (v1)

ContentScore =
  completion_rate × 50
+ avg_watch_time × 30
- skip_rate × 40
+ rewatch_rate × 20

    This ensures great videos win, even from small creators.

LAYER 3 — Instagram Social Logic (Relationship Boost)

After content quality, apply social bonuses.

If followed creator → +30
If mutual follow → +20
If friend liked it → +10

This ensures:

    Friends don’t disappear

    Feed feels personal

    Social trust is preserved

LAYER 4 — Game Mechanics Boost (Your Secret Weapon 🏆)

This is what Instagram & TikTok don’t have.
League-Based Boost

If creator league > viewer league → +15
If same league → +10

Challenge-Based Boost

If short is in active challenge → +30
If challenge nearing deadline → +10 urgency boost

This creates:

    Competitive tension

    Time pressure

    Repeat engagement

LAYER 5 — Controlled Randomness (Anti-Bubble)

TikTok ALWAYS injects randomness.

5–10% of feed = random high-quality shorts

Purpose:

    Prevent echo chambers

    Discover new interests

    Test new creators

LAYER 6 — Final Score Formula

FinalScore =
  ContentScore        (TikTok)
+ SocialScore         (Instagram)
+ GameScore           (Your App)
+ FreshnessScore
- FraudPenalty

Sorted DESC → feed.
PART 2 — Cold-Start Rollout Logic (VERY IMPORTANT)

Cold start applies to:

    New shorts

    New creators

    New users

A) Cold Start for NEW SHORTS (TikTok-style rollout)
Phase-Based Distribution
PHASE 1 — Micro Test (50–100 users)

Conditions:

    Random users

    Similar interest group

    Mixed leagues

Metrics collected:

    Completion rate

    Skip rate

    Likes per view

    Watch time

If completion_rate ≥ 70%
→ Phase 2
Else → Kill or slow

PHASE 2 — Expanded Test (1k–5k users)

Now include:

    Interest-matched users

    League-based users

    Some followers

Thresholds:

completion_rate ≥ 60%
engagement_rate ≥ X

PHASE 3 — Scale or Freeze
Result	Action
Excellent	Trending bucket
Average	Normal distribution
Poor	Demoted
Go Pseudocode

func RolloutStage(short Short) int {
    views := short.Views
    cr := short.CompletionRate

    if views < 100 {
        return 1
    }
    if views < 5000 && cr > 0.7 {
        return 2
    }
    if cr > 0.6 {
        return 3
    }
    return 0
}

B) Cold Start for NEW CREATORS

Instagram problem → TikTok solution.
Rule

    Creator follower count NEVER blocks reach

Instead:

    First 5–10 shorts get boosted testing

    Compare creator to peers (same league)

NewCreatorBoost = +20 for first N shorts

C) Cold Start for NEW USERS
First 20 shorts feed mix:

40% trending
30% random high-quality
20% challenge content
10% social

After 20 shorts → personalization kicks in.
PART 3 — Why This Actually Works

✔ TikTok logic → viral growth
✔ Instagram logic → retention & trust
✔ Game logic → addiction & competition
✔ Cold-start → fair to new users
✔ Rule-based → debuggable
✔ ML-ready → future-proof

   ↓

   ↓

Just tell me 🔥

So if a user upload short do we make a score for each short and how can we update it again and again like if someone watches it. Like what

This is a very important question, and you’re thinking exactly like someone building a real Shorts system 👍
I’ll explain this very clearly and practically, without ML first, and in a way that fits your Go + Redis + DB architecture.
Short Answer (Big Picture)

YES
👉 Every short has a score
👉 The score is NOT static
👉 The score is continuously updated as users interact
👉 You do NOT recalculate everything from scratch

Instead:

    You store metrics

    You update aggregates

    You recompute score incrementally

Mental Model (IMPORTANT)

Think of a short like a live stock price 📈

    Upload → initial price

    Every watch/like → price moves

    Viral → price explodes

    Skipped → price drops

You don’t recalc the whole market every second.
STEP 1 — What You Store for EACH SHORT
In Database (Postgres / Mongo)

short {
  id,
  user_id,
  created_at,
  duration,

  views,
  likes,
  comments,
  shares,

  total_watch_time,
  completion_count,
  skip_count,

  rollout_stage,    // 1, 2, 3
  fraud_score
}

STEP 2 — Initial Score at Upload

When a user uploads a short:

BaseScore = FreshnessBoost + NewCreatorBoost

Example:

BaseScore = 30

Store it:

short.score = 30

STEP 3 — When Someone Watches a Short (MOST IMPORTANT)

You DO NOT recalculate full score.

You update counters, then adjust score.
Event: User watches short
1️⃣ Update counters

views += 1
total_watch_time += watch_time

If watched > 80%:

completion_count += 1

If watched < 20%:

skip_count += 1

2️⃣ Compute delta (score change)

completion_rate = completion_count / views
avg_watch_time = total_watch_time / views
skip_rate = skip_count / views

3️⃣ Incrementally adjust score

score += (watch_time_ratio × 2)

if completed:
  score += 5

if skipped:
  score -= 8

⚠️ Notice:

    Small increments

    Smooth changes

    No heavy computation

STEP 4 — When Someone Likes / Comments / Shares

These are strong signals, so bigger boosts.

Like     → +6
Comment  → +10
Share    → +15

But apply diminishing returns:

boost = base_boost / log(likes + 2)

This prevents spam likes.
STEP 5 — Time Decay (Score Should Fade)

Every hour/day:

score = score × decay_factor

Example:

decay_factor = 0.97 per hour

This ensures:

    Old shorts fade

    New shorts surface

STEP 6 — Cold-Start Rollout Uses Views, Not Score

Rollout stage is based on views + completion rate, NOT score.

views < 100           → Stage 1
views 100–5,000       → Stage 2 (if CR ≥ 70%)
views > 5,000         → Stage 3

Score helps ranking within each stage.
STEP 7 — Where Do We Store Score? (IMPORTANT)
Redis (HOT DATA 🔥)

short:score:{short_id} → float
short:metrics:{id}     → counters

Used for:

    Feed ranking

    Fast sorting

Database (COLD DATA ❄️)

    Persist every X minutes

    Used for recovery

    Analytics

STEP 8 — What Happens When Many Users Watch?
Example Timeline
Event	Views	Score
Upload	0	30
10 good watches	10	55
50 mixed watches	60	62
20 skips	80	45
Viral wave	500	120

Score breathes.
STEP 9 — Feed Ranking (IMPORTANT)

When building feed for a user:

FinalFeedScore =
  short.score              (global quality)
+ user_specific_boost
+ social_boost
+ game_boost

You DO NOT store user-specific score.

That would explode memory.
STEP 10 — Why This Design Is CORRECT

✅ Scales
✅ Real-time
✅ No recomputation
✅ Easy ML upgrade
✅ Easy fraud detection

This is exactly how TikTok-style systems work (simplified).
STEP 11 — Very Common Mistake (AVOID ❌)

❌ Recalculating score from scratch per request
❌ Storing score per user per short
❌ Heavy DB joins per scroll
TL;DR

    Yes, each short has a score

    Score updates incrementally

    Update on every interaction

    Store hot data in Redis

    Decay over time

    Rank using global + user boosts

Just tell me 👍

so every interaction with short we'll update the what happens like, views, comments, etc. and we calculate score for each value change

Yes — exactly 👍
Every interaction with a short updates its counters, and from those changes you adjust the score incrementally.

Let me walk you through this cleanly and precisely, so there’s no confusion about what happens on each interaction and why this scales.
The Correct Mental Model

    Interactions update metrics → metrics slightly adjust score

You are NOT:

    recalculating full score every time

    looping through all metrics every request

You are doing small score deltas per event.
What Happens on EACH Interaction (Event-Driven)

Think in terms of events, not API calls.
1️⃣ VIEW (User opens short)
What you update

views += 1

Score change

score += +0.5

Why?

    View alone is weak signal

    Prevents fake view spam

2️⃣ WATCH TIME (User watches X seconds)

This is the strongest signal.
What you update

total_watch_time += watch_time

If watch_time ≥ 80%:

completion_count += 1

If watch_time ≤ 20%:

skip_count += 1

Score change

watch_ratio = watch_time / duration

score += watch_ratio × 2

if completed:
  score += +5

if skipped:
  score -= -8

3️⃣ LIKE
What you update

likes += 1

Score change

score += 6 / log(likes + 2)

Why log?

    Prevents like farming

    Early likes matter more

4️⃣ COMMENT
What you update

comments += 1

Score change

score += 10 / log(comments + 2)

Comments = effort → strong signal
5️⃣ SHARE
What you update

shares += 1

Score change

score += 15 / log(shares + 2)

Shares = external validation → VERY strong
6️⃣ NEGATIVE SIGNALS (IMPORTANT)
Instant skip (e.g. <2 seconds)

skip_count += 1
score -= 12

Repeated skips by many users

score *= 0.9

This kills bad shorts fast.
Where This Logic Lives (Architecture)
Interaction Flow

User watches short
   ↓
Flutter sends interaction event
   ↓
API Gateway (Node)
   ↓
Go Interaction Service
   ↓
Update Redis counters
   ↓
Apply score delta
   ↓
Async persist to DB

Redis Structure (Example)

short:metrics:{id}
  views
  likes
  comments
  shares
  total_watch_time
  completion_count
  skip_count

short:score:{id} → float

Redis is fast + atomic.
Time Decay (Runs Periodically)

Even if no one watches:

Every 1 hour:
  score = score × 0.97

This is done by:

    cron job

    background worker

Cold-Start Interaction Handling

For new shorts, early interactions matter more.

If views < 100:
  score_delta *= 1.5

This helps discovery.
Feed Ranking Uses This Score

When building feed:

rank_score =
  global_short_score
+ social_boost
+ interest_boost
+ game_boost

You do NOT recompute global score here.
Why This Is the Right Way

✔ Real-time
✔ Scales to millions
✔ No DB bottleneck
✔ Easy ML upgrade
✔ Fraud detection friendly
Common Mistake (Don’t Do This ❌)

❌ “On every view, recalculate score from all metrics”
❌ “Store per-user score per short”
❌ “Heavy SQL queries per scroll”

    “so every interaction with short we’ll update views, comments, etc. and calculate score?”

✅ Yes
✅ Update metrics
✅ Apply small score delta
✅ Store in Redis
✅ Decay over time

Just tell me 👍

instead of calculating score for every likes, views, etc. I should make something like a excel sheet and where i add columns and views, likes, comments and after every increment score update and that short comes top. 

You’re very close, and your Excel-sheet analogy is actually correct 👍
Let me refine it so you don’t fall into a common trap.
Short Answer

✅ YES, think of it like an Excel row per short
❌ NO, don’t recompute the whole “formula” cell every time from scratch

Instead:

    You maintain columns (metrics)
    and update one “Score” column incrementally

The Correct “Excel Sheet” Model

Imagine each short is one row:
short_id	views	likes	comments	shares	watch_time	skips	score
s1	120	15	4	2	980s	6	73.4
s2	80	5	1	0	430s	20	22.1

This is exactly what you are doing, just not in Excel — in Redis + DB.
What Happens on Interaction (Excel Thinking)
Example: A new LIKE on s1
Step 1 — Update column

likes = likes + 1

Step 2 — Update score cell (delta)

score = score + Δlike

You are not recalculating:

score = f(views, likes, comments, shares, ...)

every time.

You’re doing:

score += small_change

Why You Should NOT Recalculate Like Excel Formula

If you do this on every interaction:

❌ Expensive
❌ Hard to scale
❌ DB heavy
❌ Race conditions

TikTok/Instagram do not do this.
The Right Way (Incremental Formula)

Instead of:

score = views*0.5 + likes*6 + comments*10 + shares*15

You do:

On view      → score += +0.5
On like      → score += +6/log(likes+2)
On comment   → score += +10/log(comments+2)
On share     → score += +15/log(shares+2)
On skip      → score -= 8

This is equivalent, but scalable.
Sorting = “Excel Sort by Score”

Your feed is literally:

    Sort all rows by score DESC

Redis:

ZADD shorts:ranking score short_id

Top shorts = ZRANGE ... REV
Where This Excel Sheet Lives
Hot Sheet (Real-Time)

Redis

    Fast

    Sorted sets

    Atomic increments

Cold Sheet (Backup)

Postgres / Mongo

    Persist periodically

    Analytics

    Recovery

Very Important Optimization
You do NOT have one global Excel sheet only

You actually have multiple logical sheets:

    Trending shorts

    Challenge shorts

    League-based shorts

    Fresh shorts

Each has its own ranking set.
Example Redis Structures

short:metrics:s1 → hash
short:score:s1   → float

shorts:trending  → ZSET
shorts:challenge:123 → ZSET

When Score Changes, What Happens?

    Update short:score:s1

    Update sorted sets where it belongs

    Redis automatically reorders it

Just like Excel auto-sorting.
Common Mistake (DON’T DO THIS ❌)

❌ One massive SQL table
❌ ORDER BY score every request
❌ Full recompute on each event
Final Clear Answer to Your Thought

    “I should make something like an excel sheet and add columns and after every increment score update and that short comes top”

✅ Conceptually YES
✅ Practically YES
⚠️ But use incremental updates, not full recalculation
⚠️ Use Redis sorted sets, not DB ORDER BY

You are thinking in the right direction.

how we monitor watch time? tell me in short and easy language

Sure — short and easy 👍
How watch time is monitored (simple version)
1️⃣ Video starts

When the short starts playing, the app:

    notes the start time

    starts a timer

2️⃣ Video keeps playing

While the video is:

    on screen

    not paused

    app is in foreground

the timer keeps running.
3️⃣ Video stops / user scrolls

When the user:

    scrolls away

    closes the app

    pauses the video

the app:

watch_time = current_time − start_time

4️⃣ App sends watch time to backend

Flutter sends something like:

{
  "short_id": "s1",
  "watch_time": 6.8,
  "video_duration": 10
}

5️⃣ Backend decides what it means

Backend checks:

    watched ≥ 80% → completed

    watched ≤ 20% → skipped

    else → normal view

Then updates:

    watch time

    completion count

    skip count

    score

Very important rules (to avoid fake watch time)

    Count only when video is visible

    Ignore when app is in background

    Cap watch time at video duration

    Ignore super-fast scrolls (<1–2 sec)

One-line summary

    Watch time = how long the video stayed playing on the user’s screen

That’s it.

If you want, next I can show:

    Flutter code idea

    How TikTok avoids fake watch time

    How to track re-watches

    How to batch events (to save API calls)

Just tell me 👍

how tiktok avoids fake watch time

I’ll explain this very simply, no jargon, no ML talk unless needed.
Big idea (1 line)

    TikTok does NOT trust one signal.
    It combines many small checks to decide if a watch is real.

1️⃣ Screen visibility (most important)

TikTok counts watch time only if:

    video is on screen

    video is actually playing

    user is not scrolling

    app is in foreground

❌ If app is minimized → no watch time
❌ If video is hidden → no watch time
2️⃣ Minimum watch threshold

Very short views are ignored.

Example:

    watched < 1–2 seconds → not counted

    fast scrolls → ignored

This kills scroll-bots.
3️⃣ Watch pattern check (human behavior)

Humans:

    watch unevenly

    pause

    rewatch

    scroll at random times

Bots:

    watch exact duration

    repeat perfectly

    constant speed

TikTok flags too-perfect patterns.
4️⃣ Completion rate vs likes mismatch

Example:

    90% completion

    but almost no likes/comments

⚠️ Suspicious.

Real users usually:

    like/comment when they enjoy

    or skip early

TikTok cross-checks signals.
5️⃣ Device & session signals

TikTok looks at:

    device ID

    OS version

    screen size

    session length

If:

    thousands of views from same device type/IP
    → downgrade or ignore them.

6️⃣ Speed of interaction (timing)

Fake behavior:

    like at exact same timestamp

    comment instantly every time

TikTok adds random delay checks.
7️⃣ Rewatch behavior

Real users:

    sometimes rewatch

    sometimes stop early

Bots:

    rewatch same pattern

TikTok values natural rewatch variance.
8️⃣ Trust-based scoring (important)

Every user/device has a trust score.

New or suspicious accounts:

    their watch time = low weight
    Trusted users:

    their watch time = high weight

So fake accounts barely affect score.
9️⃣ Gradual rollout protects system

New video is shown to small group first.

If early data looks fake → video dies early.

This prevents mass fake boosting.
10️⃣ What YOU should copy (simple version)

For your app, do this 👇
Count watch time only if:

    video visible

    app foreground

    watched ≥ 2 seconds

Ignore or reduce weight if:

    too many views from same IP/device

    no likes/comments after many completions

    perfect watch pattern

Give trust weight:

real_watch_time = watch_time × user_trust_score

One-line summary

    TikTok avoids fake watch time by trusting behavior, not numbers.

    Give simple Flutter logic to track real watch time

    Design trust score system

    Show Redis schema

    Explain re-watch handling

    Show how bots fail silently

Great question 👍

I’ll explain it clearly, step by step, in easy language.
Big Idea (1 line)

    Category = best guess at upload → corrected by user behavior later

You don’t need to be perfect at upload.
STEP 1 — Categories You Should Have (Simple)

Example:

    Comedy

    Dance

    Music

    Tech

    Education

    Motivation

    Lifestyle

    Travel

    Food

Broad categories work best.
STEP 2 — How Category Is Set at Upload (3 signals)

    Creator knows content best

This is where TikTok becomes smart.

After users watch:
If most viewers are:

    gaming lovers → gaming

    comedy lovers → comedy

You adjust category confidence, not hard switch immediately.
Example Data Stored

short_category_scores: {
  "gaming": 0.7,
  "comedy": 0.2,
  "sports": 0.1
}

If gaming keeps winning → stays gaming.
STEP 5 — Multi-Category Support (Advanced but Useful)

Shorts can belong to multiple categories.

Example:

    Gaming + Comedy

    Tech + Education

Use weights:

Gaming (70%), Comedy (30%)

Feed system uses the highest match.
STEP 6 — What If Creator Lies About Category?

No problem.

If creator selects:

Category: Education

But viewers:

    skip fast

    gaming users like it

System slowly:

    reduces Education weight

    increases Gaming weight

No punishment needed.
STEP 7 — Cold-Start Category Handling

For new short:

    Test in selected category

    Also test in General

Whichever performs better wins.

    Interest-based feed

    Cold-start testing

    Discover tab

    Ads later

    Category is an initial guess that gets corrected by how people watch it.

Just tell me 👍

my app is like someone challenge another guy may be a friend, in his league, etc. 
so they will both make short, photo etc. how can i make sure user watches both the videos in order to judge it or app can judge based on user response as well. But if i make like tiktok or instagram, or facebook, like they scroll upward they will not see other video. This is for challenge videos not for feed algorithm i am making.

Got it — this is challenge judging logic, NOT feed logic.
And you’re right: normal TikTok-style scrolling breaks fair judging.

So the solution is:

    Challenge Mode ≠ Feed Mode

I’ll explain how big apps solve this, and then give you a clean, fair system you can actually build.
The Core Problem (Your Exact Concern)

    Two users upload challenge entries

    Judge must see BOTH

    Order must be controlled

    Scrolling must not skip

    Judgment must be fair

👉 This means you CANNOT use normal vertical feed behavior
The Correct Mental Model

    A challenge is a comparison flow, not a feed

Think:

    TikTok feed → discovery

    Challenge → match / duel / face-off

SOLUTION OVERVIEW (High Level)

You create a special Challenge Viewer, with:

    Forced viewing

    Controlled order

    Explicit judging action

    Optional audience voting

OPTION 1 — FORCED SEQUENTIAL VIEWING (Most Fair ✅)
UX Flow

Challenge Opened
   ↓
Video A plays (cannot skip)
   ↓
Video B plays (cannot skip)
   ↓
Judge / Vote screen

Key Rules

    User cannot scroll

    User cannot vote until both are watched

    Watch completion is mandatory

How to Enforce It (Simple Rules)
Frontend

    Disable swipe

    Disable back until both watched ≥ X%

    Lock next button until completion

Backend

Store flags:

challenge_view_progress {
  user_id,
  challenge_id,
  watched_a: true,
  watched_b: true
}

Vote allowed only if:

watched_a == true AND watched_b == true

Why This Works

✔ Fair
✔ No cheating
✔ Simple
✔ Easy to explain to users
OPTION 2 — SIDE-BY-SIDE / TOGGLE VIEW (Very Popular)

Used in:

    Talent apps

    Dance battles

    Gaming clips

UX

[ Video A ]  <— Toggle —>  [ Video B ]

Rules:

    User must watch both tabs

    Track watch time per tab

    Vote unlocks only after both viewed

This feels less forced, but still fair.
OPTION 3 — SPLIT-SCREEN (Advanced)

┌──────────┬──────────┐
│ Video A  │ Video B  │
└──────────┴──────────┘

Best for:

    Dance

    Singing

    Fitness

Harder technically, but very fair.
HOW THE APP JUDGES (Backend Logic)

You should combine three signals:
1️⃣ Audience Vote (Primary)

After watching both:

Who performed better?
[ A ] [ B ]

Store:

vote {
  challenge_id,
  voter_id,
  voted_for
}

2️⃣ Engagement Metrics (Secondary)

Track per entry:

    Avg watch time

    Completion rate

    Likes/comments (inside challenge only)

Use this if:

    Votes are tied

    Or no votes

3️⃣ Anti-Bias Protection (IMPORTANT)
Randomize order

Some users see:

A → B

Others see:

B → A

This prevents:

    First-video advantage

Store:

viewer_order: "AB" | "BA"

HOW TO PREVENT SKIPPING & CHEATING
Don’t count if:

    watched < 70%

    app in background

    video muted + minimized

    instant vote

Penalize:

    same IP voting many times

    bot-like voting speed

CHALLENGE SCORING EXAMPLE

Final Score =
  (Audience Votes × 60%)
+ (Avg Watch Time × 25%)
+ (Likes/Comments × 15%)

Winner = higher score.
IMPORTANT: THIS SHOULD NOT AFFECT FEED SCORE

Challenge videos:

    Have separate metrics

    Separate ranking

    Separate logic

Do NOT mix with feed scoring.

3️⃣ Vote unlock after both watched

if toggle left and right can i stop mobile sending back from my app if someone do back because most of the phone now toggle left and right from corner to take back to home screen.

Short answer: you can’t fully stop system gestures, but you can design around them safely.
Big apps (TikTok, YouTube, games) do gesture containment, not blocking.

I’ll explain clearly and practically, for Android + iOS, and what YOU should do for challenge mode.
Important truth (must understand first)

    ❌ You CANNOT disable system back/home gestures
    ✅ You CAN intercept, delay, warn, or recover from them

OS gestures always win. Apps that try to block them get rejected.
What actually happens on phones
Android

    Swipe from left/right edge → Back

    Swipe from bottom → Home

iOS

    Swipe from left edge → Back

    Swipe from bottom → Home

    Swipe down → App switcher

What TikTok / Games actually do (secret)

They don’t block gestures
They protect state + resume safely
✅ Correct Solution for Your Challenge Toggle Screen

You should implement 4 layers of protection
1️⃣ Edge-safe UI (MOST IMPORTANT)
DO NOT place toggle controls near edges

❌ Bad:

|<  Video A   |   Video B  >|

✅ Good:

      Video A
   [ ← Switch → ]
      Video B

Leave 24–32dp padding from left/right edges.

This alone avoids 80% accidental exits.
2️⃣ Intercept BACK button (Android)

You CAN intercept back inside the app.
Behavior you want:

    If challenge not finished → confirm exit

    If finished → allow exit

Logic:

If watched both videos == false
→ show confirmation dialog
Else
→ allow back

This is allowed and common.
3️⃣ Detect app going background (VERY IMPORTANT)

You cannot stop home gesture, BUT:

You CAN detect when it happens.
When app goes background:

    Pause challenge

    Save progress

    Mark challenge as "incomplete"

When user comes back:

"Please complete both videos to vote"
Resume where they left

This is EXACTLY how games do it.
4️⃣ Lock voting unless BOTH watched

Even if user:

    exits

    backgrounds

    reopens

Backend rule stays:

vote_allowed =
  watched_A >= 70%
  AND
  watched_B >= 70%

So escaping gives no advantage.
How TikTok-style apps handle this (real behavior)

    If you leave mid-video → watch time stops

    If you return → video resumes

    If you swipe home → nothing is counted

They don’t fight the OS.
UX Pattern You Should Copy (Best Practice)
Challenge Screen Rules

    No scrolling

    No edge controls

    Clear progress indicator:

Video A ✔   Video B ⏳

On exit attempt:

"You must watch both entries to judge this challenge."
[ Continue ] [ Exit ]

What NOT to do ❌ (Very important)

❌ Trying to disable gestures
❌ Using hacks / overlays
❌ Preventing home button
❌ Forcing fullscreen without exit

These cause:

    Bad UX

    App store rejection

    User rage 😅

Final Clear Answer to Your Question

    “Can I stop mobile back/home gestures if I use left-right toggle?”

❌ You cannot stop them
✅ You design around them
✅ You protect state
✅ You enforce rules on backend

That’s how real apps do it.
Recommended Setup for YOU (Challenge Mode)

✔ Center toggle buttons
✔ Edge padding
✔ Back confirmation
✔ Resume on return
✔ Vote unlock only after both watched

This gives fair judging without fighting the OS.

This is a very good question, and you’re thinking at the right level now — not just engineering, but human psychology + OS behavior.
I’ll answer this honestly, like a product designer + psychiatrist + mobile engineer.
First: Is your idea bad?

No.
But in its current form, it has high frustration risk.

Let me explain why, then I’ll give you a much better pattern that big apps and games actually use.
Why Your Current Idea Can Make Users Hate the App 😬

You proposed:

    Tap → next challenge

    Scroll up → Video B

    Scroll up again → next challenge

    Scroll left → next challenge

Psychologically, this causes 3 problems:
1️⃣ Gesture overload (cognitive stress)

Users must remember:

    What scroll up does now

    What scroll left does

    What tap does

Humans hate mode switching without visual cues.

Result:

    “Why did it go to next challenge?? I didn’t want that.”

That creates loss of control, which causes rage quits.
2️⃣ OS gesture conflict (big red flag 🚨)

    Left swipe = back (Android/iOS)

    Bottom swipe = home

When your app sometimes:

    goes next challenge

    sometimes exits app

The brain experiences unpredictability.

Unpredictable UI = anger, not engagement.
3️⃣ Accidental punishment feels unfair

Imagine:

    User accidentally scrolls left

    Challenge skipped

    Progress lost

Psychologically:

    “The app punished me for a mistake.”

This kills trust.
What TikTok / Games Know (Psychology Rule)

    Never punish a gesture mistake.

If a mistake happens:

    App should recover

    Or ask confirmation

    Or make it reversible

✅ A MUCH BETTER IDEA (Recommended)
Principle:

    One gesture = one meaning

No overloading.
🟢 Best UX Pattern for Challenge Mode (Psych-safe)
1️⃣ Scroll UP = switch between A ↔ B ONLY

    Up/down toggles only between challenge entries

    No skipping challenges

    No feed behavior

This is safe and familiar.
2️⃣ Tap (center button) = Next Challenge

    Clear button:

[ NEXT CHALLENGE ]

No hidden behavior.
3️⃣ Long-press = Skip (optional)

    Hold 1–2 seconds

    Show confirmation:

Skip this challenge?

Long-press is intentional → less accidental.
4️⃣ Swipe LEFT = disabled or hint only

Do NOT map left swipe to navigation.

Instead:

    Ignore it

    Or show hint:

"Use Next button to continue"

This avoids OS conflict.
What About Fingerprint-style Touch?
This is good ONLY if:

    It is visual

    It is optional

    It is reversible

Example:

[ Tap & Hold to Continue ]

Used in:

    Games

    Meditation apps

    Duels

But NOT for core navigation.
Ideal Challenge Flow (Psychologically Safe)

Challenge X
  ↓
Scroll ↑ → Video A
Scroll ↑ → Video B
  ↓
Vote unlocked
  ↓
Tap [Next Challenge]

Why This Works (Psychiatrist POV)

✔ Predictable
✔ No surprise exits
✔ No OS gesture conflict
✔ User feels in control
✔ Mistakes are harmless

Control = comfort
Comfort = retention
What Big Apps Avoid (On Purpose)

❌ Using left/right swipe for navigation
❌ Overloading gestures
❌ Gesture-only navigation
❌ Hidden behavior

Even TikTok avoids horizontal swipe for main actions.
Final Clear Recommendation

    ❌ Don’t overload gestures
    ❌ Don’t fight OS gestures
    ✅ One gesture, one meaning
    ✅ Use visible buttons for progression
    ✅ Make mistakes reversible

Your instinct is right — users WILL hate gesture confusion.

2️⃣ USER CONTROL TOLERANCE (POWER VS COMFORT)

Some users:

    Love chaos & randomness

    Others hate unpredictability

You partially cover this with random %, but not fully.
🔥 Add:

control_preference_score
algorithm_resistance_score
exploration_fatigue_rate

This tells you:

    How much freedom vs curation to give

    When to slow down recommendations

You track wins/losses, but not reaction to losing.

Two users can lose:

    One becomes addicted

    One rage quits forever

🔥 Add:

loss_tolerance_score
post_loss_engagement_drop
revenge_play_probability

This is extremely important for a challenge-based app.
4️⃣ REWARD SATURATION (DOPAMINE BURNOUT)

You track dopamine release, but not diminishing returns.

Same content that worked yesterday:

    May stop working today

    May cause fatigue

🔥 Add:

reward_decay_rate
content_staleness_score
dopamine_saturation_level

This helps you rotate content before burnout happens.
5️⃣ IDENTITY ALIGNMENT (WHY user comes back)

This is deep — but extremely powerful.

People don’t return only for dopamine.
They return because:

    “This app understands who I am”

Examples:

    “I am a competitor”

    “I am funny”

    “I am skilled”

    “I am improving”

🔥 Add:

identity_cluster
identity_reinforcement_score
identity_conflict_score

If content breaks identity → user leaves.
6️⃣ SOCIAL COMPARISON SENSITIVITY

Challenges amplify comparison.

Some users:

    Thrive on seeing better players

    Others quit if shown too strong opponents

🔥 Add:

comparison_tolerance
upward_comparison_limit
downward_boredom_threshold

7️⃣ CREATOR VS CONSUMER SWITCHING

Many users are both.

Your algorithm must know:

    When to push creation

    When to push consumption

🔥 Add:

👉 Key idea
The feed is not driven by content.
It is driven by mental state alignment.
2️⃣ FEED DECISION ENGINE (How content is chosen)
Step 1: Read user snapshot (real-time)

UserSnapshot {
  intent_state
  emotional_wave
  fatigue_level
  identity_cluster
  social_pressure
}

Step 2: Decide feed mode

IF fatigue > threshold → Recovery Mode
ELSE IF intent == compete → Challenge Mode
ELSE IF intent == chill → Comfort Mode
ELSE → Explore Mode

Step 3: Decide emotional pacing

MAX same-emotion shorts = emotional_saturation_limit
INSERT contrast content every N items

Example:

Comedy → Comedy → Motivation → Comedy → Neutral

Step 4: Content scoring (simplified)

final_score =
  relevance_score
+ identity_alignment
+ intent_match
- fatigue_penalty
- avoidance_penalty
+ novelty_bonus
+ social_proof_weight

Step 5: Ethical gate (VERY IMPORTANT)

Before showing content:

IF overstimulation_risk == HIGH
   ↓ reduce intensity
IF ego_exploitation_score > limit
   ↓ balance with grounding content
IF loss_streak + frustration > safe_limit
   ↓ avoid hard challenges

3️⃣ AVOIDING ETHICAL DARK PATTERNS 🚨

This is where most apps fail.
❌ Dark patterns to avoid
Pattern	Why harmful
Endless intensity escalation	Burns dopamine
Ego-only reinforcement	Narcissistic loop
Punishment through boredom	Emotional manipulation
Forced streak anxiety	Addiction pressure
✅ Ethical safeguards (YOU SHOULD ADD)
1. Dopamine governor

dopamine_budget_per_session

Once exceeded → soften feed
2. Loss compassion rule

IF user loses repeatedly:
  show supportive / skill-building content

3. Identity respect

Never show content that humiliates user identity
4. Fatigue transparency

Subtle UX hints:

    Slower feed

    Natural pauses

    No “one more” tricks

4️⃣ STEP-BY-STEP LEARNING ALGORITHM (CORE LOGIC)
Phase 1: Cold Start (Day 0–2)

Goal: Learn avoidance first

Track:
- Skip speed
- Dislike signals
- Watch completion

Phase 2: Emotional Mapping (Day 2–5)

Detect:
- Emotional response clusters
- Energy preference
- Stimulation tolerance

Phase 3: Identity Detection (Day 5–10)

Infer:
- Competitor vs Viewer
- Creator vs Consumer
- Ego sensitivity

Phase 4: Intent Prediction (Ongoing)

Per session:

Predict intent within first 5 actions
Adjust feed within 3 items

Phase 5: Long-Term Optimization

Optimize for:
- Return probability
- Emotional stability
- Growth, not dependency

Perfect question.
I’ll do this very carefully and in very easy language, because if this part is wrong, the whole algorithm becomes fantasy.

I’ll follow this structure for every item:

    What it is (in human terms)
    How to store it (simple numbers / buckets)
    How to find / update it (from user actions)

No math heavy stuff. No ML jargon unless needed.
PART 1 — BASIC WATCH METRICS (FOUNDATION)
1️⃣ watch_completion_rate

What it is
How much of a video the user usually watches.

Store

0.0 → 1.0

Example: 0.72 = watches 72% on average

How to find

watched_seconds / total_video_seconds

Average this per user over last N videos.
2️⃣ avg_watch_time

What it is
How long (in seconds) the user watches a video on average.

Store

seconds (number)

How to find
Add watched time for all videos → divide by number of videos watched.
3️⃣ rewatch_rate

What it is
Does the user re-watch content?

Store

0.0 → 1.0

How to find

videos_watched_more_than_once / total_videos

Rewatch = strong interest.
PART 2 — EMOTION (INFER, DON’T ASK)
4️⃣ mood / emotion right now

What it is
Temporary emotional state in this session.

Store

ENUM or label
{ calm, excited, competitive, bored, frustrated }

How to infer
From last 3–5 actions:

    Fast swipes → bored

    Long watch + likes → engaged

    Aggressive challenges → competitive

    Rage quit → frustrated

⚠️ Never permanent.
5️⃣ last emotion

What it is
Emotion from previous session.

Store

same enum as above

How to infer
Whatever emotion was dominant when last session ended.
PART 3 — FATIGUE & ENERGY (VERY IMPORTANT)
6️⃣ fatigue_threshold

What it is
How much content the user can consume before getting tired.

Store

number of videos

How to find
Point where:

    watch time drops

    skip speed increases

    likes stop

That number = fatigue threshold.
7️⃣ content_energy_preference

What it is
Does the user like calm or high-energy content?

Store

LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH

How to find

    Watches comedy calmly → LOW

    Likes loud, fast, hype videos → HIGH

8️⃣ fatigue_score

What it is
Current tiredness level right now.

Store

0 → 100

How to find
Increase with:

    long sessions

    repeated emotions
    Decrease after:

    breaks

    calm content

PART 4 — DOPAMINE & EGO
9️⃣ ego_validation_score

What it is
How much the user likes praise, winning, attention.

Store

0.0 → 1.0

How to find

    Likes leaderboards

    Replays wins

    Shares achievements

🔟 dopamine_response_score

What it is
How strongly user reacts to rewarding content.

Store

0.0 → 1.0

How to find
Measure spike in:

    watch time

    likes

    replays
    after exciting content

PART 5 — NEGATIVE SIGNALS (MOST IMPORTANT)
11️⃣ content he avoid

What it is
Content types user consistently skips.

Store

list of tags

How to find
If skipped <2s multiple times → avoid.
12️⃣ skip speed

What it is
How fast user swipes away.

Store

seconds

How to find
Time between video start and swipe.
13️⃣ fast swipe rate

What it is
How often user swipes immediately.

Store

percentage

How to find

fast_swipes / total_videos

14️⃣ rage quit trigger

What it is
What makes the user close the app.

Store

reason label

How to find
Last content before app close + behavior pattern.

cluster labels

Store

0 → 100

How to find

    Account age

    No spam

17️⃣ pattern of feed

How feed is composed.

Store

percentages

Example:

trending 50%
friends 30%
random 20%

How to find

    Same tag shown repeatedly

    Watch completion decreases

    Skip speed increases

What it is
Same emotional tone shown repeatedly.

How to find
Track emotion label of last N videos:

comedy, comedy, comedy, comedy → count = 4

User is mentally overloaded.

How to find
If:

    fatigue_score high

LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH
